6.24: yevarechecha YHWH veyishmerecha yaer YHWH panav eleicha vichuneka yissa YHWH panav eleicha veyasem lecha shalom: The Aaronic Blessing (Birkat Kohanim) is the oldest liturgical text in continuous use — fragments of it were found on silver amulets from the 7th century BCE (the Ketef Hinnom scrolls, the oldest biblical text discovered). Its three-part structure increases in length: 3 words, 5 words, 7 words, with a crescendo toward shalom (peace/wholeness/well-being). The divine name YHWH appears three times — early Christian interpreters saw this as a Trinitarian hint. The blessing has been recited in Jewish synagogues for over 2,500 years and by Christian ministers at service benedictions, making it one of the most-spoken texts in human history.
6.1: YHWH's word comes to Moses with a new category of voluntary consecration — the Nazirite law. The formal address pattern (waydabber YHWH el-Moshe lemor) signals legislation rather than narrative, placing the Nazirite institution on par with priestly and sacrificial law.
6.2: נָזִיר (nāzîr) derives from the root נזר (nzr), meaning to separate, dedicate, or consecrate oneself. The cognate noun נֵזֶר (nēzer) means crown or consecration-mark (see v7), showing that Nazirite status carries a visible dignitary dimension. The vow is open to both men and women (îš ô ʾiššâ), unusually egalitarian in ancient law, creating a temporary lay priestly status.
6.3: The first prohibition is total abstention from grape products, including שֵׁכָר (šēkār) — strong drink, likely barley beer — alongside יַיִן (yayin), wine. The MKT renders this distinction as wine/beer. The prohibition extends to wine vinegar and beer vinegar: even fermented derivatives are banned. The comprehensiveness signals that the issue is not intoxication alone but total separation from the vine as a category.
6.4: The exhaustive list continues: fresh grapes, dried grapes (raisins), grape seeds (ḥarṣan), and grape skin/husks (zāg). The specificity ensures no technical loophole. This total-abstinence-from-the-vine pattern echoes the Rechabites (Jer 35) and anticipates the eschatological vine abstinence Jesus declares before the Last Supper (Mark 14:25). The Nazirite's vine-separation embodies a particular holiness-orientation: belonging entirely to YHWH rather than to the ordinary pleasures of Canaan.
6.5: The second prohibition: no razor may touch the Nazirite's head during the vow period. The uncut hair is explicitly called נֵזֶר (nēzer) — the same word for the golden diadem worn by the high priest (Exod 29:6; Lev 8:9) and for the royal crown. The Nazirite's flowing hair is a visible crown of consecration, marking dedicated status the way priestly vestments mark ordination. He is qādôš — holy to YHWH — throughout the vow period.
6.6: The third prohibition: no contact with a נֶפֶשׁ מֵת (nep̱eš mēt), a dead person. The phrase uses nep̱eš (soul/life) rather than gûp̄ (body), emphasizing the entire personal reality of the deceased. This prohibition matches the high priest's restriction (Lev 21:11) — not merely the ordinary priestly restriction of avoiding all but close family (Lev 21:1-3). The Nazirite assumes, for the vow period, the purity standard of Israel's highest office.
6.7: Even parents, siblings, or children — the close family for whom ordinary priests may defile themselves (Lev 21:2-3) — are off-limits to the Nazirite. The reason given: kî nezer ʾĕlōhāyw ʿal-rōʾšô — because the crown of his God is upon his head. The nēzer (hair-crown) embodies the divine claim on the Nazirite; severing this relationship, even for grief, would violate the consecration. Personal love must yield to higher consecration — a pattern that appears again in Jesus's teaching about discipleship and family ties (Matt 10:37).
6.8: The Nazirite is declared qādôš — holy — for the entire period of separation. Holiness in Hebrew thought is not primarily a moral predicate but a relational one: belonging to YHWH, set apart from ordinary use. The Nazirite's holiness is temporary but total, encompassing diet, hair, and purity — a comprehensive bodily consecration.
6.9: Accidental defilement — someone dying suddenly in his presence — voids the entire accumulated period. The language veṭimmēʾ rōʾš nizrô — he defiles his consecrated head — shows the hair (crown) as the locus of consecration. Defilement requires shaving the head on the seventh day (the standard purification completion day) and presenting it for burning, eliminating the consecration-symbol entirely.
6.10: On the eighth day — the day of new beginnings across sacrificial law (Lev 8:33; 9:1; 14:10,23; 15:14,29) — the Nazirite brings two turtledoves or two young pigeons. This is the minimum-cost offering (used for the poor in Lev 5:7; 12:8), suggesting the provision includes those of limited means.
6.11: One bird serves as חַטָּאת (ḥaṭṭāʾt) — sin offering — and one as עֹלָה (ʿōlâ) — burnt offering. The priest makes atonement for the Nazirite, with the explicit purpose stated: ʾăšer ḥāṭāʾ ʿal-hannāp̱eš — because he sinned by reason of the dead person. The accidental defilement, though involuntary, requires atonement — a key principle of the sacrificial system: moral intent and ritual state are both real categories requiring different responses.
6.12: After atonement, the Nazirite reconsecrates his days to YHWH and brings a אָשָׁם (ʾāšām) — guilt/reparation offering — of a year-old male lamb. The previous days of the vow are yipplû — they fall, are forfeited. The ʾāšām compensates for the breach of sacred obligation; it is the offering for unauthorized use of holy things (Lev 5:14-6:7). The void-and-restart structure means the Nazirite's consecration period cannot be partially valid — it is all-or-nothing.
6.13: The completion ceremony begins when the full vow term is satisfied. The Nazirite is brought to the tent of meeting — the ceremony is formal and communal. zōʾt tôrat hannāzîr — this is the law/instruction of the Nazirite — frames what follows as comprehensive legislation for the concluding rites.
6.14: Three animals form the core of the completion offering: (1) a male lamb without blemish (תָּמִים, tāmîm) for the burnt offering, (2) a female lamb without blemish for the sin offering, (3) a ram without blemish for the fellowship offering. The use of tāmîm — the same term used for the Passover lamb (Exod 12:5) and throughout Levitical sacrificial law — signals that the Nazirite's completion offering meets the highest standard of unblemishedness.
6.15: The grain and drink accompaniments follow the fellowship offering: a basket of unleavened bread (loaves and wafers), grain offering, and drink offerings. The full complement of offerings — burnt, sin, fellowship, grain, drink — represents the complete sacrificial repertoire, making the Nazirite's completion a comprehensive act of worship.
6.16: The priest officiates the sin and burnt offerings on the Nazirite's behalf. The sin offering precedes the burnt offering — standard sequence in Levitical ritual: atonement enables ascent (the burnt offering = total dedication ascending to God).
6.17: The ram as fellowship (שֶׁלֶם, šelem) offering — the communal meal offering shared between YHWH, priest, and worshiper — forms the climax of the completion ceremony. The basket of unleavened bread accompanies it. The fellowship offering celebrates the restored or completed relationship; completing a vow is precisely the occasion it serves (Lev 7:16).
6.18: The Nazirite's consecrated hair (rōʾš nizrô — the head of his consecration) is shaved at the tent of meeting entrance. The hair is placed under the fellowship offering pot and burned. This act consummates the vow: the nēzer (consecration-mark) is offered to YHWH in the fire. The Nazirite's separation is ended not by mere completion of time but by a ritual act that transfers the embodied consecration to YHWH.
6.19: The priest performs the wave offering with three items: the boiled shoulder of the ram, an unleavened loaf, and a wafer. The shoulder — a priestly portion under fellowship offerings (Lev 7:32-33) — is here waved along with bread before being given to the priest. This distinguishes the completion ceremony from a standard fellowship offering by the additional ritual action.
6.20: After the wave offering, it becomes qōdeš (holy portion) for the priest, alongside the wave breast and contribution thigh. Following this, the Nazirite may drink wine. The explicit permission signals that the wine-abstinence was the visible marker of the vow, and lifting it marks the definitive return to ordinary status. The entire sequence — shaving, offering, priestly portion, permission to drink — is a formal ceremony of return.
6.21: The concluding rubric summarizes: this is the law of the Nazirite who makes a vow. It adds a qualifying principle: the required minimum can be exceeded — whatever else his resources allow. Vow law generally holds that voluntary pledges can go beyond the minimum; the law establishes a floor, not a ceiling. The phrase al-pi nidrô — according to his vow — makes the entire law dependent on the sincere fulfillment of whatever was specifically pledged.
6.22: The Aaronic blessing (vv22-27) begins with a second revelation formula, signaling a distinct legal unit appended to the Nazirite legislation. The two laws share thematic connection: the Nazirite law ends with the priest conferring sacred portions; immediately following, the priests receive the formula by which YHWH himself blesses through their speech.
6.23: Aaron and his sons are commanded to bless the Israelites with this specific formula. The verb בָּרַךְ (bārak) in the Piel = actively confer blessing; this is performative speech, not a wish. The priests are the agents, but v27 clarifies that YHWH is the effective cause. The formula uses three couplets with increasing length, each beginning with the divine name YHWH and containing 3, 5, and 7 words respectively in Hebrew — a carefully structured oral text.
6.25: yāʾēr YHWH pānāyw ʾēleykā wîḥunneka — May YHWH make his face shine on you and be gracious to you. פָּנִים (pānîm) — face — is the Hebrew idiom for personal presence, character, and favor. A shining face (ʾôr pānîm) signifies delight and approval (Ps 31:16; 80:3,7,19; 119:135). חנן (ḥānan) — be gracious — describes sovereign, unearned favor; חֵן (ḥēn) is the relational grace underlying covenant relationship. The face-shining idiom appears in Moses's prayer for divine presence after the golden calf (Exod 33:18-23), where seeing the face is the fullest possible intimacy with God.
6.26: yiśśāʾ YHWH pānāyw ʾēleykā weyāśēm ləkā šālôm — May YHWH lift up his face toward you and grant you peace. נָשָׂא פָּנִים (nāśāʾ pānîm) — to lift the face toward someone — means to give full favorable attention, the opposite of turning away or hiding the face (Ps 102:2; 143:7). שָׁלוֹם (šālôm) — peace — encompasses far more than absence of conflict: completeness, wholeness, flourishing of every relationship. It is the summary term for the totality of covenant blessing (Num 25:12; Isa 54:10; Ezek 34:25).
6.27: weśāmû ʾet-šəmî ʿal-bənê yiśrāʾēl waʾănî ʾăbārekēm — They shall put my name on the Israelites, and I will bless them. The priests' recitation of the three-fold formula puts the divine name on the people — blessing is the act of naming, of declaring whose they are. YHWH appears three times in vv24-26, once per couplet; the three-fold invocation of the Name is the structural engine of the blessing. The final clause shifts to first person singular — I will bless them — YHWH claiming direct agency over what the priests pronounce. Priestly mediation and divine immediacy are not in tension; the priest's words are the vehicle of YHWH's own action.

21.8: veasa lecha saraf veshim oto al nes vehaya kol hanashuch veraah oto vachai: 'Make a fiery serpent and set it on a pole, and everyone who is bitten, when he sees it, shall live.' The serpent-on-a-pole raises a question: is this a violation of the second commandment (no graven images)? The text suggests: (1) it is the looking in faith, not the object itself, that saves; (2) YHWH appointed the image for a specific purpose as a means of grace. The Greek translation (ophis chalkous, bronze serpent) uses the same word Paul uses in 2 Cor 5:21: God made him who knew no sin to be sin (Gk. hamartia) for us — Christ becomes the thing that kills (sin/the serpent) in order to be the means of salvation for all who look to him.
21.1: vayishma haKena'ani melech-Arad yoshev haNegev: 'when the Canaanite, the king of Arad, who lived in the Negev, heard.' The first military engagement after Aaron's death: Israel encounters Canaanite opposition from the south. Arad's king hears that Israel is coming by the 'way of Atharim' (perhaps the spies' route from Num 13). The proactive Canaanite attack — taking some prisoners — provokes Israel's vow.
21.2: vayiddar Yisrael neder laYHWH vayomar im-naton titen et-ha'am hazeh beyadi vehacharamti et-areihem: 'and Israel vowed a vow to YHWH and said: If you will indeed give this people into my hand, then I will devote their cities to destruction [cherem].' Vayiddar neder (vowed a vow) — the cognate construction for emphasis. Cherem (irrevocable devotion/destruction) — total consecration to YHWH of the conquered cities; nothing kept for Israel. The vow conditions military success on YHWH's faithfulness and Israel's total renunciation of spoil. The site is named Chormah (destruction) after the fulfillment (v3).
21.3: vayishma YHWH bekol Yisrael vayiten et-haKena'ani vayacharem otam ve'et-areihem vayikra shem-hamakom Chormah: 'YHWH heeded the voice of Israel and gave over the Canaanites, and they devoted them and their cities to destruction. So the name of the place was called Hormah.' Vayishma YHWH bekol: YHWH hears the voice of Israel — the prayer is heard. Chormah (from cherem): the place-name preserves the vow's fulfillment. This first land victory after a generation of failure anticipates the coming conquest.
21.4: vayis'u meihor hahar derech Yam-Suf lisbot et-eretz Edom vatiketzer nefesh ha'am baderech: 'they journeyed from Mount Hor by the way to the Red Sea, to go around the land of Edom. And the people became impatient on the way.' Vatiketzer nefesh ha'am: literally 'the soul/spirit of the people became short' — katzer (short, cut short) applied to nefesh (soul/spirit/life). The idiom for impatience, frustration, discouragement: the spirit becomes 'too short' for the burden of the journey. The circuitous route around Edom shortens Israel's spiritual patience.
21.5: vayedaber ha'am be'Elohim uveMoshe lamah he'elitunu mimitzrayim lamut bamidbar ki ein lechem ve'ein mayim venafshenu katzah balachem hakelokelt: 'the people spoke against God and against Moses: Why have you brought us up out of Egypt to die in the wilderness? For there is no food and no water, and we loathe this worthless food.' Venapshenu katzah balachem hakelokelt: 'our soul loathes/is disgusted with this contemptible food.' Kelokelt (contemptible/miserable/light) — a dismissive term for the manna. The people who once complained there was no food now despise the food YHWH provides. The escalation of complaint is notable: rejection of the gift itself.
21.6: vayeshalach YHWH ba'am et hanechashim hasrafim vayyinashu et-ha'am vayamot am-rav miYisrael: 'YHWH sent fiery serpents among the people, and they bit the people, so that many people of Israel died.' Nechashim srafim (fiery/burning serpents): saraf means both 'burn' and is related to seraphim (the burning/fiery ones of Isa 6:2). The serpent sent as divine judgment on the manna-despising generation bites with fire. The connection to Eden's serpent is unmistakable: the original instrument of the fall returns as the instrument of covenant judgment.
21.7: vayavo ha'am el-Moshe vayomru chatanu ki-dibarnu veYHWH uvach hitpalel el-YHWH veyasar me'alenu et-hanachash vayitpalel Moshe ba'ad ha'am: 'the people came to Moses and said: We have sinned, for we have spoken against YHWH and against you. Pray to YHWH, that he take away the serpents from us. So Moses prayed for the people.' Chatanu (we have sinned) — the sin is named. The pattern of judgment-confession-intercession-provision recurs from ch11 (Taberah, v1-3) and ch16 (Korah plague, 16:46-48). Moses's intercession is immediate and unrecorded — the prayer is brief; the provision is elaborate.
21.9: vaya'as Moshe nachash nechoshet vayesimehu al hanes vehayah im-nasach hanachash et-ish vehibit el-nachash hanechoshet vachai: 'Moses made a bronze serpent and set it on a pole. And if a serpent bit anyone, he would look at the bronze serpent and live.' The fulfillment of the look-and-live provision. Al hanes (on the pole/standard): the nes (pole/sign/banner/miracle) on which the serpent is raised is the same word used for military standards and miracles throughout Numbers. The image raised on the pole — which brings healing to those who look — becomes the explicit type Jesus names: 'As Moses lifted up the serpent in the wilderness, so must the Son of Man be lifted up, that whoever believes in him may have eternal life' (John 3:14-15).
21.10: vayisu vnei Yisrael vayachanu be'Ovot: 'the people of Israel set out and camped at Oboth.' The journey resumes after the bronze serpent episode. The itinerary list (vv10-20) preserves the route through Transjordan with various stopping points. The names encode the wilderness experience: Ovot may connect to ov (spirit/ghost/necromancer) — the wilderness place-names often carry etymological memory.
21.11: vayisu me'Ovot vayachanu be'Iye-ha'Avarim bamidbar asher al-penei Moav mimizrach hashemesh: 'they set out from Oboth and camped at Iye-abarim, in the wilderness that is opposite Moab, toward the sunrise.' Iye-ha'Avarim (ruins of the Abarim/crossings): the Avarim (those who cross over) are mentioned — this region borders the Jordan crossing territory. Mimizrach hashemesh (toward the sunrise) — the east/sunrise orientation marks progress toward Canaan.
21.12: misham nas'u vayachanu benachal Zared: 'from there they set out and camped in the Valley of Zered.' Nachal Zered (Wadi Zered) — the traditional border between Edom/Moab territory; crossing the Zered marks a significant geographical transition in the wilderness journey (Deut 2:13-14 identifies crossing the Zered as the moment the military generation finished dying).
21.13: misham nas'u vayachanu me'ever ha'Arnon asher bamidbar hayotze migvul ha'Emori ki Arnon gvul Moav bein Moav uvein ha'Emori: 'from there they set out and camped on the other side of the Arnon, which is in the wilderness that extends from the boundary of the Amorites. For the Arnon is the boundary of Moab, between Moab and the Amorites.' Nachal Arnon (the Arnon River/valley) — the border between Moab (south) and the Amorite kingdom of Sihon (north). The journey is now in the territory that Israel will need to traverse by defeating Sihon (vv21-32).
21.14: al ken ye'amar besefer milchamot YHWH et-Vaheb beSufah venathalim Arnon: 'therefore it is said in the Book of the Wars of YHWH: Waheb in Suphah, and the valleys of the Arnon.' Sefer milchamot YHWH (Book of the Wars of YHWH) — an ancient Israelite source text now lost; cited here for the geographical/military record. The existence of this book suggests that Numbers draws on archival sources; it preserves a fragment of early Israelite military poetry.
21.15: ve'eshed hanechalim asher natah leshevet Ar venishaan ligvul Moav: 'and the slope of the valleys that extends to the seat of Ar, and leans to the border of Moab.' The geographical detail of the journey route; the archival fragment continues with place-names along the Arnon valley.
21.16: umiSham Be'erah hi habe'er asher amar YHWH leMoshe esof et-ha'am ve'etenah lahem mayim: 'and from there they continued to Beer; that is the well about which YHWH said to Moses: Gather the people together, so that I may give them water.' Beer = well. YHWH provides water again — this time without the Meribah failure; the provision is gracious, the response is poetic.
21.17: az yashir Yisrael et-hashira hazot ali ve'er enu-lah: 'then Israel sang this song: Spring up, O well! — sing to it!' Az yashir (then sang) — the same introduction as the Song of the Sea (Exod 15:1). This brief well-song is Israel's second wilderness song. Ali ve'er (spring up, O well): the well is addressed directly as if it has agency. Paul later identifies the wilderness water-source as Christ: 'they drank from the spiritual Rock that followed them, and the Rock was Christ' (1 Cor 10:4).
21.18: be'er charuha sarim kafruha nedivei ha'am bimechoqek bemish'anotam: 'a well that the princes dug, that the nobles of the people delved, with the scepter and with their staffs.' The leaders dig the well; the mechoqeq (scepter/lawgiver) and mish'anot (staffs) are used as digging tools. The imagery of rulers digging the well is unusual; it may encode the tradition of Moses striking the rock (now transformed into collaborative well-digging) or it may be a literal memory of a communally dug cistern.
21.19: umiMidbar Matanah umiMatanah Nachaliel umiNachaliel Bamot: 'and from the wilderness to Mattanah, and from Mattanah to Nahaliel, and from Nahaliel to Bamoth.' The place-names are themselves theological: Mattanah = gift; Nachaliel = river/stream of God; Bamot = high places. The journey passes through places named Gift → River of God → High Places: a theological topography.
21.20: umiVamot hagei asher bisadeh Moav rosh hapisgah venishkafah al-penei hayeshimon: 'and from Bamoth to the valley in the region of Moab by the top of Pisgah that overlooks the desert.' Rosh hapisgah (the top of Pisgah) — this peak will appear again in the Balaam oracles (Num 23:14) and as Moses's death location (Deut 34:1). The itinerary positions Israel at the height overlooking the Jordan valley — the land is visible from here.
21.21: vayishlach Yisrael mal'achim el-Sichon melech ha'Emori lemor: Israel sends messengers to Sihon king of the Amorites. The diplomatic protocol mirrors the Edom request (20:14-21): the same language, the same King's Highway, the same promises. But Sihon is not a brother-nation — the Amorites have no covenantal claim against Israel's passage.
21.22: e'evrah ve'artzecha lo nitteh besadeh uvecherem lo nishteh mei be'er derech hamelech nelech ad asher na'avor gvulecha: 'let me pass through your land. We will not turn aside into field or vineyard. We will not drink the water of a well. We will go by the King's Highway until we have passed through your territory.' The identical diplomatic formula used with Edom — word for word. The comparison is deliberate: Israel makes the same request, but Edom (a brother) and Sihon (an Amorite) both refuse. The Amorite refusal, unlike Edom's, leads to war.
21.23: velo-natan Sichon et-Yisrael avor bigvulo vaye'esof Sichon et-kol-ammo vayetze likrat Yisrael hamilbarah: 'but Sihon did not allow Israel to pass through his territory. He gathered all his people together and went out against Israel to the wilderness.' Hamilbarah (to the wilderness/desert) — Sihon comes out to attack in the open desert terrain. Deuteronomy 2:30 adds: 'YHWH your God hardened his spirit and made his heart obstinate, that he might give him into your hand.' Sihon's refusal is the divinely-ordained trigger for Israel's first major land victory.
21.24: vayakkehu Yisrael lefi-charev vayirash et-artzo miArnon ad-Yabok: 'Israel struck him with the edge of the sword and took possession of his land from the Arnon to the Jabbok.' Lefi-charev (at the mouth of the sword) — the sword's edge expressed as its mouth (peh, the same word as mouth for speech and water-giving). Vayirash (took possession) — the verb of covenant inheritance: Israel takes possession (yarash) of the first Transjordanian territory. The Arnon to Jabbok boundary becomes Israelite territory.
21.25: vayikach Yisrael et kol-he'arim ha'eleh vayeshev Yisrael bekol-arei ha'Emori beCheshbon uvechal-banotehah: 'Israel took all these cities, and Israel settled in all the cities of the Amorites, in Heshbon, and in all its villages.' Vayeshev (settled/dwelt) — Israel inhabits the Amorite cities. This is the first settlement in territory east of the Jordan — the beginning of the conquest pattern.
21.26: ki Cheshbon ir Sichon melech ha'Emori hi vehu nilcham bemelech Moav harishon vayikach et-kol-artzo miyado ad-Arnon: 'for Heshbon was the city of Sihon the king of the Amorites, who had fought against the former king of Moab and taken all his land from him as far as the Arnon.' Historical context: Heshbon had been Moabite before Sihon conquered it. Israel conquers what was Moab's former territory through Sihon — an indirect inheritance through conquest.
21.27: al-ken yomru hamoshlim bo'u Cheshbon tibbaneh vetikonen ir Sichon: 'therefore the ballad singers say: Come to Heshbon, let it be built; let the city of Sihon be established.' A poetic fragment from the moshlim (those who compose proverbs/songs) celebrating Sihon's former victory — which Israel now inherits. The taunt song against Moab (vv27-30) was originally a celebration of Sihon's conquest of Moab; Israel now sings it as their own victory song, having defeated Sihon.
21.28: ki esh yatze'ah miCheshbon lehabah mir'kiryat Sichon achlah Ar Moav ba'alei bamot Arnon: 'for fire came out from Heshbon, flame from the city of Sihon. It devoured Ar of Moab, and swallowed the heights of the Arnon.' The taunt poem: fire/flame imagery for military conquest. Esh... lehabah (fire... flame) — two terms for the unstoppable advance. Moab's destruction by Sihon (now inherited by Israel's victory) is described in fire-language that resonates with the divine fire motif throughout Numbers.
21.29: oy-lecha Moav avadeta am Kemosh natan banav pelitim uvenotav bashevut lemelech Emori Sichon: 'woe to you, O Moab! You are undone, O people of Chemosh! He has made his sons fugitives, and his daughters captives, to an Amorite king, Sihon.' Chemosh — Moab's national deity. The taunt names Chemosh as the failed divine protector: 'his sons became fugitives... to an Amorite king.' YHWH defeats the nations by defeating their gods. Oy-lecha (woe to you) — the prophetic woe oracle applied in taunt poetry.
21.30: vanniram avad Cheshbon ad-Divon vanavashim ad-Nofa asher ad-Medeivah: 'we overthrew them; Heshbon, as far as Dibon, perished; and we laid waste as far as Nophah, which reaches to Medeba.' The conclusion of the taunt song. The place names — Dibon, Nophah, Medeba — mark Moabite territory now claimed by Israel's advance. The song ends with comprehensive destruction.
21.31: vayeshev Yisrael be'eretz ha'Emori: 'Israel settled in the land of the Amorites.' The settlement verb (vayeshev) repeats from v25: the conquest becomes habitation. The land east of the Jordan is now Israelite territory, claimed by victory over Sihon.
21.32: vayishlach Moshe laragel et-Yazer vayilkedu et-benotehah vayorish et-ha'Emori asher-sham: 'Moses sent to spy out Jazer, and they captured its villages and dispossessed the Amorites who were there.' Laragel (to spy out) — a small reconnaisance that contrasts with the catastrophic spying of Numbers 13; this time, the spying leads immediately to capture, not despair.
21.33: vayifnu vaya'alu derech haBashan vayetze Og melech haBashan likratam hu vekhol-ammo lamilchamah beEdre'i: 'they turned and went up by the way of Bashan. And Og the king of Bashan came out against them, he and all his people, to battle at Edrei.' Og of Bashan — the giant king (Deut 3:11: his iron bed was 9 cubits long) whose kingdom extends north of Gilead. His coming out against Israel repeats Sihon's pattern.
21.34: vayomer YHWH el-Moshe al-tira oto ki veyad'cha natattihu ve'et-kol-ammo ve'et-artzo: 'YHWH said to Moses: Do not fear him, for I have given him into your hand, and all his people, and his land.' Al-tira (do not fear) — the divine assurance before battle. YHWH's word precedes the victory: the outcome is given before the fight. The same formula recurs throughout Joshua (1:9; 10:8) and becomes the foundation for Israel's conquest confidence.
21.35: vayakku oto vet-banav vekhol-ammo ad-bilti hish'ir-lo sarid vayirshu et-artzo: 'and they struck him and his sons and all his people, until he had no survivor left. And they possessed his land.' Ad-bilti hish'ir-lo sarid: 'until no survivor remained' — the totality of the cherem-pattern victory. Vayirshu (they possessed) — the same possession-verb as v24. The double conquest of Sihon and Og east of the Jordan establishes the Transjordanian foothold from which Joshua will launch the main Canaan campaign. Moses's sermon in Deuteronomy returns to these two victories as the paradigm for Israel's faith in YHWH's conquest promises.

1.1: vayedaber YHWH el-Moshe bemidbar Sinai be'ohel mo'ed: 'YHWH spoke to Moses in the wilderness of Sinai, in the tent of meeting.' The book of Numbers opens at the intersection of three theological locations: midbar (wilderness — the in-between place, neither Egypt nor the land), Sinai (the mountain of covenant), and ohel mo'ed (the tent of meeting — the completed tabernacle from which YHWH now speaks, Exod 40:34-35). The divine speech originates from the tabernacle's interior: YHWH who filled the tent with his glory now issues orders from within it. 'The first day of the second month in the second year' places this one month after the tabernacle's completion (Exod 40:17).
1.2: seu et rosh: 'lift the head of' — the Hebrew idiom for conducting a census. Nasa (lift) + rosh (head) = the counting metaphor: each person's head is lifted/registered. The book's Hebrew name Bemidbar (in the wilderness) is preferred over the Greek 'Numbers' (arithmoi) derived from this census. The count proceeds lemishpechotam lebeit avotam (by their clans, by their fathers' houses) — two levels of social organization: mishpacha (extended family/clan) and beit av (father's house, the nuclear family unit). Legulgelotam: by their heads/skulls (from gulgolet = skull; the same root as Golgotha, 'place of the skull').
1.3: kol yotzei tzava be-Yisrael: 'all who go out to war in Israel' — the military framing of the census. Yotzei tzava (those who go out of the host/army) defines the counted group: adult males 20 years and older capable of military service. Tzava (host/army) is also the word behind YHWH tzvaot (LORD of hosts) — the cosmic military title. Israel's earthly army reflects the divine heavenly host. Tifkedu otam le-tzivotam: 'you shall enroll them by their companies/armies' — the corporate military unit (tzava) is the organizing principle.
1.4: ve'itchem yihyu ish ish lamatteh: 'with you shall be a man from each tribe' — one leader per tribe. Ish ish (each man / a man, a man) is the emphatic distributive construction stressing individual responsibility. Matteh (staff/tribe) — the tribe takes its name from the leader's staff; matteh means both 'rod/staff' (the leader's symbol) and 'tribe' (those grouped under that staff). The dual meaning preserves the organic connection between leader and community.
1.5: ve'eleh shemot ha-anashim asher ya'amdu itchem: 'and these are the names of the men who shall assist you.' Ya'amdu (shall stand/assist) — the verb amad (stand) expresses readiness and appointment; standing before someone = serving them. The leader 'stands with' Moses and Aaron in the census-taking. Lir'uven (for Reuben): Elitzur ben Shedeur — 'my God is a rock, son of the shedder of light.' The names are theophoric; each leader's name reflects covenant theology.
1.6: lishim'on: for Simeon — Shelumi'el ben Tzurishaddai: 'my peace is God, son of the rock of Shaddai.' Shaddai (the Almighty/El Shaddai) appears in the father's name; the Shaddai title, prominent in Genesis, continues through the tribal lineage.
1.7: liYhudah: for Judah — Nachshon ben Aminadav: 'serpent (or diver), son of the willing people.' Nachshon ben Aminadav will later be identified in rabbinic tradition as the first to step into the Red Sea. His name appears in the genealogy of David (Ruth 4:20) and in Matthew's genealogy of Jesus (Matt 1:4). The Judahite leader stands in the direct ancestral line of the Messiah.
1.8: liYissachar: for Issachar — Netanel ben Tzuar: 'God has given, son of the rock.' The gift-name (natan el) reflects the covenant blessing. Issachar's tribe will later be noted for 'understanding of the times' (1 Chr 12:32).
1.9: liZevulun: for Zebulun — Eli'av ben Chelon: 'my God is father, son of strength.' Av (father) in the leader's name reflects the patriarchal theology: God as father of Israel. Zebulun's territory will later encompass the Galilee region where Jesus began his ministry (Matt 4:13-16, citing Isa 9:1-2).
1.10: livnei Yosef: 'for the sons of Joseph' — Joseph's double-tribe inheritance (Ephraim and Manasseh) is listed under his name as the parent tribe. le-Efrayim: Elishama ben Ammihud: 'my God has heard, son of my people is glorious.' liMenashe: Gamliel ben Pedahtzur: 'God has rewarded, son of redeemed by the rock.' The el (God) and tzur (rock) elements in names continue the creedal pattern.
1.11: liBinyamin: for Benjamin — Avidan ben Gid'oni: 'my father is judge, son of my cutter/warrior.' Benjamin's leader is the only one whose father-name references a warrior (gid'on — the same root as the judge Gideon). Benjamin later produces Israel's first king (Saul) and Paul the apostle.
1.12: leDan: for Dan — Achi'ezer ben Ammishaddai: 'my brother is help, son of the people of Shaddai.' Dan's leader carries a name of brotherly solidarity (ach = brother; ezer = help). Dan will later become associated with northern apostasy but here stands in covenant solidarity.
1.13: le-Asher: for Asher — Pag'iel ben Ocheran: 'God met/encountered me, son of the troubled.' Paga (to meet, encounter, light upon) is a verb with a range from accidental meeting to intentional encounter with God; this leader's name commemorates a divine encounter. The el suffix makes it theophoric.
1.14: leGad: for Gad — Elyasaf ben Re'u'el: 'God has added, son of shepherd/friend of God.' Re'u'el is the same name as Jethro's alternate name (Exod 2:18; Num 10:29) — 'friend of God' (re'a el) or possibly 'shepherd of God' (ra'ah el).
1.15: leNaftali: for Naphtali — Achira ben Enan: 'my brother is evil/shepherd (?), son of the gracious.' Naphtali, whose territory will also be part of the Galilean region (Isa 9:1), is represented by a leader whose name contains ach (brother) — the fraternal solidarity motif recurring in the tribal leadership roster.
1.16: eleh keruyei ha'edah nesiei mattot avotam: 'these are those called of the congregation, the princes of the tribes of their fathers.' Nesiim (princes) — from nasa (lift); the nasi is the 'lifted one' — lifted above his tribe in honor and responsibility. Mattot avotam (tribes of their fathers) — each nasi represents the patriarchal lineage. The double designation — called of the congregation (keruyei ha'edah) + princes of tribes — marks their dual accountability: elected by the community, representing the ancestral line.
1.17: va-yikach Moshe ve-Aharon et ha-anashim ha-eleh asher nikvu beshemot: 'Moses and Aaron took these men who had been designated by name.' Nikvu beshemot (designated/pierced by names) — naqav (to specify, designate, pierce) applied to naming; to be 'named' in this context is to be officially designated. The names are not incidental; each person is individually identified in the census.
1.18: ve-et kol-ha'edah hikehilu be-echad lachodesh hasheni: 'they assembled all the congregation on the first of the second month.' Hakhel (convene/assemble) — from kahal (assembly, congregation); the census assembles the entire community. Vayityaldu (they declared their genealogies) — yalad (to give birth/genealogize); the census is a genealogical act, rooting each individual in family lineage.
1.19: ka'asher tzivah YHWH et Moshe vayifkedem bemidbar Sinai: 'as YHWH commanded Moses, he enrolled them in the wilderness of Sinai.' The census (paqad) proceeds in exact obedience to divine command. Bemidbar Sinai (in the Sinai wilderness) — the census is wilderness-situated; this is not an enrollment of settled people but of a pilgrim community en route.
1.20: vayihyu vnei Reuven bechor Yisrael: 'the sons of Reuben, the firstborn of Israel.' Reuben is enumerated first as Jacob's eldest. Each tribe's formula begins: ldorotam lemishpechotam lebeit avotam bemispar shemot lgulgelotam — 'by their generations, by their clans, by their fathers' houses, by the number of names, head by head.' The fourfold enumeration structure runs through every tribe: generation, clan, father's house, individual name-count.
1.21: peqoudeihem lematteh Reuven shisha ve-arbaim elef vachamesh me'ot: 'those enrolled of the tribe of Reuben: 46,500.' Pequdim (those enrolled/numbered) from paqad. The number 46,500: elef may mean a military unit rather than a literal thousand in some usages; if so, the figure represents 46 units plus 500 individuals. The tribe numbers across the census are consistent with the total 603,550 in v46.
1.22: livnei Shim'on: for Simeon. The formula repeats for each tribe. Simeon's count (59,300) will decline sharply to 22,200 by the second census (Num 26:14) — the steepest drop of any tribe, likely connected to the Baal Peor incident (Num 25) where the leader Zimri (a Simeonite, Num 25:14) was killed. The census numbers are not static; they reflect covenant consequence.
1.23: peqoudeihem lematteh Shim'on tish'ah vachamishim elef ushlosh me'ot: 'those enrolled of the tribe of Simeon: 59,300.' The paqad formula concludes each tribal count. Tish'ah vachamishim (59) — Simeon's large first-census number will make the second census decline all the more striking.
1.24: livnei Gad: for Gad. Gad, though sixth in Jacob's birth order (son of Zilpah), is enumerated here third after Reuben and Simeon — possibly reflecting the camp assignment (Reuben's group, south camp) rather than strict birth order. The census ordering reflects military-organizational rather than purely genealogical priorities.
1.25: peqoudeihem lematteh Gad chamisha vechamishim elef veshesh me'ot vachamishim: 'those enrolled of the tribe of Gad: 45,650.' Gad's count. Chamishim (fifty) — the round-number components in tribal counts have led some scholars to propose that the census counts represent administrative units, not literal population figures. The debate touches elef's dual meaning.
1.26: livnei Yehudah: for Judah. Judah leads the eastern camp and will be listed first in the marching order (Num 2:3-9, 10:14). The tribal census sequence here is Reuben, Simeon, Gad (southern camp, Num 2:10-16), then Judah (eastern camp). The census ordering by camp-assignment reflects the military structure of Numbers overall.
1.27: peqoudeihem lematteh Yehudah arba'ah veshiv'im elef veshesh me'ot: 'those enrolled of the tribe of Judah: 74,600' — the largest single tribe. Judah's numerical supremacy reflects the blessing of Jacob (Gen 49:8-12) and Moses (Deut 33:7). From Judah comes the scepter that does not depart (Gen 49:10), the royal line culminating in David and the Messiah.
1.28: livnei Yissachar: for Issachar. Issachar and Zebulun bracket Judah in the eastern camp (Num 2:5-8) — they are Judah's flanking tribes in the vanguard position. Issachar is praised in the Deborah song (Judg 5:15) and later known for wisdom ('understanding the times,' 1 Chr 12:32).
1.29: peqoudeihem lematteh Yissachar arba'ah vachamishim elef ve-arba me'ot: 'those enrolled of the tribe of Issachar: 54,400.' Arba'ah vachamishim (fifty-four). The Issachar count is mid-range among the tribes.
1.30: livnei Zevulun: for Zebulun. Zebulun completes the eastern camp group with Judah and Issachar (Num 2:7). Zebulun is associated with the northern coastal trade region (Gen 49:13) and with the Galilee territory where Jesus ministered (Isa 9:1-2 → Matt 4:13-16).
1.31: peqoudeihem lematteh Zevulun shiv'ah vachamishim elef ve-arba me'ot: 'those enrolled of the tribe of Zebulun: 57,400.' Zebulun's count makes the eastern camp total (Judah + Issachar + Zebulun): 74,600 + 54,400 + 57,400 = 186,400 — the largest camp contingent.
1.32: livnei Yosef livnei Efrayim: 'for the sons of Joseph, for the sons of Ephraim.' Ephraim is listed first of the two Joseph tribes, reflecting Jacob's blessing of Ephraim over Manasseh (Gen 48:14-20: 'the younger shall be greater'). Ephraim becomes the leading tribe of the northern kingdom; the name is sometimes used as a metonym for the northern ten tribes (Isa 7:2; Hos 5:3).
1.33: peqoudeihem lematteh Efrayim arba'ah vethlashim elef vachamesh me'ot: 'those enrolled of the tribe of Ephraim: 40,500.' Ephraim's count (40,500) is smaller than Manasseh's (32,200 is actually... wait, let me check) — actually Manasseh's count will be in v35: 32,200. Ephraim (40,500) is larger, which fits the Jacob blessing precedence. In the second census (Num 26), the numbers reverse — Manasseh becomes larger. The dynamics of blessing and census interact.
1.34: livnei Menashe: for Manasseh. Manasseh, though the elder of Joseph's sons, is listed after Ephraim — reflecting Jacob's cross-handed blessing. The western camp (Num 2:18-24) groups Ephraim, Manasseh, and Benjamin; Ephraim leads this group.
1.35: peqoudeihem lematteh Menashe shnayim uthlashim elef umayatayim: 'those enrolled of the tribe of Manasseh: 32,200.' Manasseh's count is the smallest of the twelve tribes in the first census, despite being Joseph's elder son. The reversal of expected precedence (Manasseh over Ephraim → Ephraim leads) is built into the census itself. By the second census (Num 26:34), Manasseh will overtake Ephraim (52,700 vs. 32,500).
1.36: livnei Binyamin: for Benjamin. Benjamin is the youngest of the twelve sons of Jacob, born last and associated with great loss (Rachel's death, Gen 35:18-19). Benjamin is the only tribe that will remain with Judah after the division (1 Kgs 12:21). Paul identifies himself as Benjamin: 'a Hebrew of Hebrews; as to the law, a Pharisee' (Phil 3:5).
1.37: peqoudeihem lematteh Binyamin chamisha uthlashim elef ve-arba me'ot: 'those enrolled of the tribe of Benjamin: 35,400.' Benjamin's count (35,400) makes the western camp total: 40,500 + 32,200 + 35,400 = 108,100 — the smallest camp contingent, which fits Benjamin's status as the youngest.
1.38: livnei Dan: for Dan. Dan leads the northern camp (Num 2:25-31), which marches last as the 'rear guard.' Dan's position at the rear — the most exposed position — is significant; Dan later becomes associated with idolatry (Judg 18; 1 Kgs 12:29) and Dan's name is notably absent from the 144,000 list in Revelation 7.
1.39: peqoudeihem lematteh Dan shnayim veshish'im elef vesheva me'ot: 'those enrolled of the tribe of Dan: 62,700.' Dan's count is substantial. The northern camp total (Dan + Asher + Naphtali): 62,700 + 41,500 + 53,400 = 157,600.
1.40: livnei Asher: for Asher. Asher (son of Zilpah, Leah's maid) is flanking tribe in the northern/rear camp. The blessing of Asher (Gen 49:20: 'Asher's food shall be rich; he shall yield royal delicacies') and Moses's blessing (Deut 33:24-25: 'most blessed of sons is Asher... as your days, so is your strength') mark him as prosperous. Anna the prophetess who greeted the infant Jesus at the temple was from Asher (Luke 2:36).
1.41: peqoudeihem lematteh Asher echad ve-arba'im elef vachamesh me'ot: 'those enrolled of the tribe of Asher: 41,500.' Asher's count.
1.42: livnei Naftali: for Naphtali. Naphtali (son of Bilhah, Rachel's maid) completes the rear camp group. Naphtali's territory (northern Galilee around the Sea of Galilee) is the region where Jesus conducted the majority of his ministry. Isaiah's prophecy (9:1-2: 'the land of Zebulun and Naphtali... a great light') is cited at the beginning of Jesus's Galilean ministry (Matt 4:13-16).
1.43: peqoudeihem lematteh Naftali shloshah vachamishim elef ve-arba me'ot: 'those enrolled of the tribe of Naphtali: 53,400.' The formula closes the twelve-tribe census. Peqoudeihem (those enrolled/numbered): the paqad verb's participial form; each tribal entry climaxes with this enrollment count.
1.44: eleh hapekudim asher pakad Moshe ve-Aharon: 'these are those who were enrolled, whom Moses and Aaron enrolled.' Hapekudim... pakad: the paqad cognate construction (enrolled / who enrolled) emphasizes the divine-authorized character of the count. Venit nesiei Yisrael (and the princes of Israel with them) — the 12 tribal leaders co-authorized the census.
1.45: vayihyu kol-pekudei vnei-Yisrael leveit avotam mikol-anshei Yisrael: 'all those enrolled of the people of Israel, by their fathers' houses, from twenty years old and upward, all able to go to war in Israel.' The census is summarized before the total: enumerated by beit av (father's house), by age (20+), by military qualification. The threefold specification mirrors v2's counting method — the census is internally consistent.
1.46: vayihyu kol-hapekudim shesh-me'ot alef ushelosheth alafim wachamesh me'ot vachamishim: 'all those enrolled were 603,550.' The grand total — shesh-me'ot elef (six hundred thousand) + shelosheth alafim (three thousand) + chamesh me'ot vachamishim (550). This figure is closely tied to Exod 12:37 ('about 600,000 men on foot') and Exod 38:26 (603,550 in the half-shekel census). The elef debate: if some alafim are military units rather than thousands, the total becomes dramatically smaller. The scholarly debate remains unresolved; the text's own internal consistency suggests the numbers are meant as literal — an army of roughly 600,000.
1.47: vehaleviim lematteh avotam lo hutpakedu betocham: 'but the Levites were not enrolled among them by their tribal affiliation.' The Levite exemption from the military census is the pivot of the chapter. Lo hutpakedu (they were not enrolled/numbered) — the Levites are not paqad'd in this census. They will be numbered separately (Num 3-4) for a different purpose: not warfare but tabernacle service. The exemption distinguishes warrior-Israel from servant-Israel (the Levites).
1.48: ki diber YHWH el-Moshe lemor: 'for YHWH spoke to Moses, saying' — the divine instruction explaining the Levite exception. The census exception is not administrative but theological: Levites belong to a different category of service than the military tribes. The ki (because/for) signals the causal basis: it is YHWH's word, not human policy, that sets apart the Levites.
1.49: ach et matteh Levi lo tifkod: 'only the tribe of Levi you shall not number.' Ach (only/but) — the contrastive particle marking the single exception to the universal census. Levi is the one tribe exempt from military enrollment, set apart for tabernacle ministry. The theological point is significant: the tribe that serves at the altar stands outside the military count — a division of labor built into the covenant community's structure.
1.50: ve-atah haf ket et-haleviyim al mishkan ha'edut: 'but appoint the Levites over the tabernacle of the testimony.' Haf ket (appoint/set): the Levites are not exempt from service but reassigned from military to sanctuary service. Mishkan ha'edut (tabernacle of the testimony) — the edut (testimony, witness) refers to the tablets of the law in the ark; the tabernacle is defined by what it houses: the divine testimony. Levites transport, assemble, and guard the tabernacle and all its vessels.
1.51: veha'zar hakorev yumat: 'and the stranger [non-Levite] who draws near shall be put to death.' Zar (stranger/unauthorized person) — not a foreigner but any Israelite who is not a Levite; zar in the priestly context means 'unauthorized' (the wrong person approaching the wrong thing). This is not hostility toward outsiders but the holiness boundary: approaching the sanctuary without proper authorization is deadly because the sanctuary is YHWH's holy space. The barrier between ordinary Israel and the sanctuary is encoded in law.
1.52: vachanah bnei Yisrael ish al-machanehu ve'ish al-diglo letzivotam: 'the people of Israel shall camp each by his own camp and each by his own standard according to their companies.' Machaneh (camp) — the military-settlement term; the camp of Israel is an organized military encampment. Degel (standard/banner) — introduced here for the first time; the tribal grouping flag. Letzivotam (by their armies/companies) — tzava again.
1.53: vehaleviim yachanu saviv lemishkan ha'edut: 'but the Levites shall camp around the tabernacle of the testimony.' The spatial arrangement: tribes camp in their four-directional groups; Levites surround the tabernacle in the center. Ve-lo yihyeh ketzef (that there be no wrath) — the Levite encirclement is a protective buffer preventing unauthorized access by non-Levites. The wrath (ketzef) that would fall on Israel if non-Levites approached is deflected by the Levite cordon.
1.54: va-ya'asu bnei Yisrael kekol asher tzivah YHWH et-Moshe ken asu: 'and the people of Israel did according to all that YHWH commanded Moses; so they did.' The closing obedience formula: ka'asher tzivah... ken asu (as commanded... so they did). Numbers 1 ends in full covenant compliance. The formula of obedience at chapter-end is a Numbers pattern that will be tested and broken in the wilderness murmurings ahead. Here, at the outset, Israel is fully obedient.

2.1: vayedaber YHWH el-Moshe ve'el-Aharon lemor: YHWH speaks to both Moses and Aaron — the census (ch1) was addressed to Moses alone; the camp arrangement is addressed to both, since both priestly and civil leadership determine the spatial arrangement of the holy community around the sanctuary.
2.2: ish al-diglo be'otot leveit avotam: 'each man by his own standard [degel], with the signs [otot] of their fathers' houses.' Degel (standard/banner) — the large tribal-group flag; ot (sign/standard) — the smaller clan-level banner. The two-level flag system mirrors the two-level organizational structure of the census (mishpacha and beit av). Mineged saviv le'ohel mo'ed yachanu: 'they shall camp facing, from all sides, the tent of meeting' — the tabernacle is the center; all four camp-directions face inward toward it. YHWH's dwelling is the centripetal force of Israel's entire spatial organization.
2.3: vehaconim kidmah mizracha degel machaneh Yehudah letzivotam: 'on the east side toward the sunrise shall be the standard of the camp of Judah by their companies.' Kidmah mizracha (eastward, toward the sunrise) — the most honorable position: the side of sunrise, the direction of the divine presence (the east-facing tabernacle entrance). Judah receives the vanguard position — first in the order of march, first in the direction of first light. This primacy of Judah (the messianic tribe) in the camp arrangement mirrors Jacob's deathbed blessing (Gen 49:8-12).
2.4: utzva'o ufekudeihem arba'ah veshiv'im elef veshesh me'ot: 'his company as enrolled: 74,600' — Judah's count repeated from ch1. The repetition confirms: the census and camp arrangement are a single coordinated system. Tzava (company/army) — each tribal group is also an tzava, an army division in the marching order.
2.5: vehachoneh alav matteh Yissachar: 'and camping next to him shall be the tribe of Issachar.' Alav (next to him/alongside him) — the flanking position; Issachar and Zebulun flank Judah in the eastern camp. The three-tribe groupings of the four camps create four mini-armies, each under a lead tribe.
2.6: utzva'o ufekudav arba'ah vachamishim elef ve-arba me'ot: 'his company as enrolled: 54,400' — Issachar's count from ch1.
2.7: matteh Zevulun: the tribe of Zebulun. Zebulun completes the eastern camp group. Nafkei orach yam is Jacob's blessing (Gen 49:13) — Zebulun's territory touches the sea; their later association with the Galilean coast region fulfills geographic destiny.
2.8: utzva'o ufekudav shiv'ah vachamishim elef ve-arba me'ot: 'his company as enrolled: 57,400' — Zebulun's count.
2.9: kol hapekudim lemachaneh Yehudah me'at shmonah veshmonim elef ve-arba me'ot letzivotam rishonah yis'u: 'all those enrolled of the camp of Judah: 186,400 by their companies. They shall set out first.' Rishonah yis'u (they shall set out/march first) — Judah leads the entire nation in the march. This vanguard position for Judah is the spatial enactment of the messianic promise: the scepter-tribe goes first, the royal lineage at the head of the covenant nation.
2.10: degel machaneh Reuven teimana letzivotam: 'the standard of the camp of Reuben on the south side.' Teiman (south) — the second camp position. Despite being Jacob's firstborn, Reuben is assigned the secondary south position rather than the primary east. Reuben lost his primacy through the sin against Bilhah (Gen 35:22; 49:3-4: 'unstable as water, you shall not have preeminence'). The census/camp arrangement encodes the covenantal consequences of the patriarchal history.
2.11: utzva'o ufekudav shisha ve-arba'im elef vachamesh me'ot: 'his company as enrolled: 46,500' — Reuben's count. Reuben's group (46,500 + 59,300 + 45,650 = 151,450) is the second-smallest camp contingent, after Ephraim's (108,100).
2.12: vehachoneh alav matteh Shim'on: 'and camping next to him shall be the tribe of Simeon.' Simeon flanks Reuben in the southern camp. Jacob's dying words to Simeon and Levi were harsh (Gen 49:5-7: 'I will divide them in Jacob and scatter them in Israel'). Simeon's numbers will collapse by the second census (from 59,300 to 22,200), and the tribe is not mentioned in Moses's final blessing (Deut 33). Levi's scattering becomes priestly blessing; Simeon's scattering becomes tribal dissolution.
2.13: utzva'o ufekudav tish'ah vachamishim elef ushlosh me'ot: 'his company as enrolled: 59,300' — Simeon's count.
2.14: umatteh Gad: and the tribe of Gad. Gad rounds out the southern camp group with Reuben and Simeon. Gad's blessing (Gen 49:19: 'raiders shall raid him, but he shall raid at their heels') suggests a warrior tribe; Gad will later settle east of the Jordan (Num 32) and be known for swift warriors (1 Chr 12:8).
2.15: utzva'o ufekudav chamisha vechamishim elef veshesh me'ot vachamishim: 'his company as enrolled: 45,650' — Gad's count.
2.16: kol hapekudim lemachaneh Reuven echad vachamishim elef ve-arba me'ot vachamishim letzivotam ushniim yis'u: 'all those enrolled of the camp of Reuben: 151,450 by their companies. They shall set out second.' Ushniim yis'u (they shall set out second) — Reuben's camp marches after Judah. The march order (east first, south second, west third, north last) maps the camp onto a counter-clockwise rotation when viewed from above.
2.17: venas'a ohel mo'ed machaneh haleviyim betoch hamachanot: 'then the tent of meeting shall set out, the camp of the Levites in the midst of the camps.' The tabernacle and its Levite carriers march in the center — between the two front camps (Judah, Reuben) and the two rear camps (Ephraim, Dan). The Levites are not a fighting unit but the center of the entire march. Ka'asher yachanu ken yis'u: 'as they camp, so they shall set out' — the marching formation exactly mirrors the camp arrangement. The holy center of Israel is always at the center, whether stationary or moving.
2.18: degel machaneh Efrayim letzivotam yama: 'the standard of the camp of Ephraim on the west side.' Yama (westward, toward the sea) — the third camp position. Ephraim's western position may reflect the tribal territory Ephraim would receive in Canaan (central highlands, western-ish). The western camp groups the Joseph tribes (Ephraim and Manasseh) with Benjamin — the three sons closest to Joseph in the Jacob narrative.
2.19: utzva'o ufekudav arba'ah vethlashim elef vachamesh me'ot: 'his company as enrolled: 40,500' — Ephraim's count.
2.20: ve'alav matteh Menashe: 'and next to him the tribe of Manasseh.' Manasseh, despite seniority, is flanking rather than leading in the Joseph camp group — the Jacob blessing precedence (Ephraim over Manasseh, Gen 48) is encoded in the camp arrangement.
2.21: utzva'o ufekudav shnayim uthlashim elef umayatayim: 'his company as enrolled: 32,200' — Manasseh's count, the smallest in the census.
2.22: umatteh Binyamin: and the tribe of Benjamin. Benjamin joins the western camp with the Joseph tribes — his brothers by Rachel. Benjamin's physical closeness to the Joseph tribes in the patriarchal narrative (Benjamin = Rachel's second son, Joseph's full brother) is preserved in the camp arrangement. The brotherhood of full siblings is honored spatially.
2.23: utzva'o ufekudav chamisha uthlashim elef ve-arba me'ot: 'his company as enrolled: 35,400' — Benjamin's count.
2.24: kol hapekudim lemachaneh Efrayim shmona vethlashim elef ume'ah letzivotam vshlishim yis'u: 'all those enrolled of the camp of Ephraim: 108,100 by their companies. They shall set out third.' Vshlishim yis'u (they shall set out third). The western camp (108,100) is the smallest of the four camps — Rachel's descendants plus Benjamin. The two sons of Jacob's beloved Rachel (Joseph/Ephraim-Manasseh and Benjamin) lead the western camp that marches third.
2.25: degel machaneh Dan tzafonah letzivotam: 'the standard of the camp of Dan on the north side.' Tzafonah (northward) — the fourth and last camp position. Dan leads the rear guard — the most rearward and exposed position. Dan's later association with idolatry and northern apostasy (Judg 17-18; 1 Kgs 12:29) may connect to this end-of-the-line position at the furthest remove from the tabernacle.
2.26: utzva'o ufekudav shnayim veshish'im elef vesheva me'ot: 'his company as enrolled: 62,700' — Dan's count. Dan is the second-largest single tribe (after Judah).
2.27: vehachoneh alav matteh Asher: 'and camping next to him the tribe of Asher.' Asher's prosperous blessing (Gen 49:20; Deut 33:24-25) and the tribe's later territory in the northern coastal plain. Anna the prophetess from Asher (Luke 2:36-38) is the only NT figure identified by Asher tribal membership.
2.28: utzva'o ufekudav echad ve-arba'im elef vachamesh me'ot: 'his company as enrolled: 41,500' — Asher's count.
2.29: umatteh Naftali: and the tribe of Naphtali, completing the northern rear-guard camp. Naphtali's blessing (Gen 49:21: 'a swift deer that yields beautiful fawns') and territory in upper Galilee (Sea of Galilee region) connect to the Galilean ministry of Jesus (Matt 4:13-16). The last camp group in the march contains the tribe whose land will be the first to see 'a great light.'
2.30: utzva'o ufekudav shloshah vachamishim elef ve-arba me'ot: 'his company as enrolled: 53,400' — Naphtali's count.
2.31: kol hapekudim lemachaneh Dan me'at vashiv'im vethlashim elef vashmona me'ot le'acharonah yis'u lediegleihem: 'all those enrolled of the camp of Dan: 157,600. They shall set out last, by their standards.' Le'acharonah yis'u (they shall set out last) — the rear guard. Lediegleihem (by their standards) — the closing phrase of the marching-order section; the degel system provides organizational identity even to those at the back.
2.32: eleh pekudei vnei Yisrael leveit avotam: 'these are those enrolled of the people of Israel by their fathers' houses.' The census summary restates the organizing principle: beit avot (fathers' houses). The entire camp is structured on patriarchal lineage — each person knows their place in the spatial arrangement because they know their tribal ancestry.
2.33: vehaleviyim lo hitpakedu betoch bnei Yisrael ka'asher tzivah YHWH et-Moshe: 'and the Levites were not enrolled among the people of Israel, as YHWH commanded Moses.' The Levite exemption from the twelve-tribe census is confirmed at the chapter's close. Ka'asher tzivah YHWH — as YHWH commanded: the exception is not an oversight but deliberate divine structuring. The Levites stand apart — spatially (at the center), functionally (sanctuary service), and statistically (not counted in the military census).
2.34: va-ya'asu bnei Yisrael kekol asher tzivah YHWH et-Moshe ken chanu letiegleihem vachen nas'u ish lemishpechotav al-beit avotav: 'the people of Israel did according to all that YHWH commanded Moses. So they camped by their standards, and so they set out, each one by his clan, according to his father's house.' The closing obedience formula mirrors ch1:54. Both the census and the camp arrangement are completed in full obedience. Ish lemishpechotav al-beit avotav (each one by his clan, according to his father's house) — the final phrase returns to the organizing categories of ch1:2. Order, genealogy, and obedience characterize Israel at Sinai — before the wilderness failures begin.

4.1: The second Levitical census of Numbers focuses on the sub-division of labor within the Levitical tribe. Chapter 3 counted all Levites from one month old; chapter 4 counts those in the prime service range (30-50 years) by sub-clan — Kohath, Gershon, Merarite — and assigns each clan its specific tabernacle-carrying duty. This organizational census is the practical implementation of Lev 8 ordination: the ordained priesthood manages the holy objects; the Levitical guilds carry them.
4.2: nasa et rosh beney Qehat: 'Take a census [lit. lift the head] of the Kohathites from among the Levites.' The Kohathites are named first because they carry the most holy objects (the ark, table, lampstand, altars). They are the most restricted in access — they cannot touch or look at the objects directly — yet they bear the most sacred burden. This inversion (greatest honor = greatest restriction + greatest danger) runs through all of Numbers 4's holiness theology.
4.3: misheloshim shanah vamalah ve'ad chamishshim shanah: 'from thirty years old and upward to fifty years old.' The age range for active Levitical service is 30-50, the prime years of physical strength. Numbers 8:24-25 gives a slightly different range (25-50) for the broader Levitical service, which may distinguish training years (25-30) from full active service. After 50 the Levite assists but no longer carries (avodah = service/work, cognate to the general labor term).
4.4: zot avodat beney Qehat be'ohel mo'ed qodesh haqqodashim: 'This is the work assigned to the Kohathites in the tent of meeting: the care of the most holy objects.' Qodesh haqqodashim (holy of holies / most holy things) — the superlative construction: the holiest of holy things. The Kohathites carry what even ordinary Israelites cannot see or touch, what even the priests approach with elaborate precaution. Their carrying responsibility is the most dangerous and the most honored of all Levitical work.
4.5: vebah Aharon uvanav: 'Aaron and his sons shall go in' — the priests enter first to wrap before the Kohathites approach. The covering protocol (described in v5-14) is exhaustive: the ark gets the veil, then a leather cover, then blue cloth; every object is wrapped in multiple layers before the Kohathites lift it. The sequence establishes a key theological principle: holy things require priestly mediation even when they are being moved. No Levite may approach the uncovered holy things.
4.6: The ark's outer covering is or tachash — translated variously as 'porpoise/dugong skin,' 'fine leather,' or 'badger skin.' The exact identification is uncertain; the Targumim translates it as the skin of a particular sea creature (sasgona). What is clear is that it is a durable, weather-resistant outer covering — the most opaque outer layer. The blue cloth placed on top (v6b, the beged techelet) identifies the object as belonging to the sanctuary. The double covering (opaque leather + identifying color) keeps both hidden and marked.
4.7: The table of the Presence (shulchan hapanim, lit. 'table of the face/presence') receives a blue cloth and all its utensils — the dishes (qe'arot), the pans (kappot), the jars (menaqiyyot), and the bowls (qesavot) used in the tamid bread ritual. The table held twelve loaves of bread representing the twelve tribes before YHWH continually (Lev 24:5-8). The detail of wrapping all the utensils individually mirrors the care given to the ark — every object associated with YHWH's presence receives proportionate reverence.
4.8: A scarlet cloth over the table's blue cloth, then the leather outer cover. The color sequence — blue (identity/holiness), scarlet (life/blood?), leather (protection) — has been variously interpreted, but the practical point is layering for both concealment and protection during wilderness transit. The NT sees the wrapping of holy things as the type of what was hidden in the OT and revealed in Christ: 'the mystery hidden for ages' (Col 1:26) is the concealed-then-revealed logic that the veiled ark enacts.
4.9: The lampstand (menorah) and all its utensils — the lamp-cups (neroteiha), the snuffers (malqacheiha), the trays (machtoteiha) — wrapped in blue cloth. The menorah is the sanctuary's light-source, the seven-branched lamp that burns perpetually (Exod 27:20). Unlike the ark, it has no poles — it is placed on a carrying frame (mot, v10). The care given to the lampstand during transit mirrors its unique function: it must arrive intact to resume its burning, the light of YHWH's presence.
4.10: venatenuh al hamot: 'they shall put it on the carrying frame.' The word mot appears only here and in v12 for carrying frames/bars used with objects that have no poles. The verb natan al (place upon) rather than the usual pole-insertion language suggests a different carrying mechanism — perhaps a stretcher-like arrangement. The precise technology is unclear; the theological point is that every holy object has a designated mode of transport, and none is dragged or left to improvisation.
4.11: The gold incense altar (mizbach hazahav) — the small altar inside the tent on which incense is burned daily and on which Yom Kippur blood is applied (Lev 4:7; 16:18-19). It receives only a blue cloth and leather cover. Notably, it does not get the scarlet middle cloth that the table gets. Each object's covering is customized to its function and position within the sanctuary hierarchy.
4.12: ucheley hasharet asher yeshartyu baqodesh: 'all the utensils of service used in the sanctuary.' The service utensils — everything used in the daily and festival rituals — are wrapped in blue cloth, covered in leather, and placed on a carrying frame. The word sharet (serve/minister) is the verb used for Levitical and priestly service throughout the OT, and its noun form mesharetim applies to both humans and the objects they use. The utensils themselves participate in the ministry.
4.13: The altar of burnt offering (mizbach ha'olah) — the large bronze altar — is cleared of ashes (dishenu, from deshen = fat/ashes of sacrifices) and covered with purple cloth (beged argaman). The purple cloth is unique to this altar among all the covered objects. Purple (argaman) is the color of kingship and royalty in the ancient world (Prov 31:22; Judg 8:26). The altar of sacrifice — where YHWH's judgment of sin is enacted — is covered in the color of royal authority.
4.14: All the utensils of the altar — the fire pans (hamachtot), the forks (hamizlagot), the shovels (hayya'im), the basins (hamizraqot) — placed on the altar and covered with leather. These are the working implements of the sacrificial system: the tools that handle burning flesh, blood, and ash. They are treated with the same care as the sanctuary's decorative furnishings — all service objects, functional or ornamental, require appropriate reverence when carried through the wilderness.
4.15: velo yig'u el haqodesh vametu: 'they shall not touch the holy things, lest they die.' The death-warning for unauthorized touching of holy objects echoes Uzzah's death at the threshing floor of Nacon (2 Sam 6:6-7) — he reached out to steady the ark and died. The holiness-danger is not incidental to the tabernacle system but central to it: YHWH's presence has a power that destroys the unprotected, the unauthorized, or the disrespectful. The carrying instructions exist because holy things can kill, not merely because they are valuable.
4.16: Eleazar ben Aharon hakohen: Eleazar son of Aaron is given supervisory oversight over the Kohathite clan's work and is personally responsible for the oil, the incense, the tamid grain offering, and the anointing oil. His role is that of the chief liaison between the high priestly family and the clan that carries the most dangerous objects. When Aaron eventually dies (Num 20:28), Eleazar becomes high priest — his administrative training in ch. 4 prepares him for this succession.
4.17: A second divine address (v17-20) focuses specifically on the danger to the Kohathites themselves. YHWH's concern is not only for the integrity of the holy objects but for the preservation of the Kohathite clan. The imperative 'do not let the Kohathite clan be cut off (yikarot) from among the Levites' (v18) reflects YHWH's pastoral concern: the protocol exists to protect those who carry the holy things, not merely to protect the holy things.
4.18: al takhritu et shevet Qehat: 'Do not cut off the clan of the Kohathites.' The verb karat (cut off) in this context means annihilation through divine judgment — the same term used for covenant-cutting and for the punishment of covenant-breakers (Lev 7:20-21). The Kohathites are at greatest risk of this judgment because they are closest to the holy things. YHWH warns Moses and Aaron not to allow circumstances to develop that would trigger this judgment — the protective protocol is not bureaucracy but pastoral care.
4.19: ze'u lahem: 'but do this for them' — Aaron and his sons are to assign each Kohathite his specific object and role before the camp moves. The personal assignment prevents two dangers: (1) confusion during transit that might lead to unauthorized contact; (2) curiosity that might lead to unauthorized looking. The phrase 'so that they may live and not die' (vechayyu velo yamuthu) is remarkable — it is addressed to the priests as a responsibility toward their subordinates. The priest's duty includes protecting those in his care from inadvertent death.
4.20: velo yavo'u lirot kebala et haqodesh vametu: 'the Kohathites themselves must not go in to look at the holy things even for a moment, or they will die.' The phrase kebala' means 'for a moment / in the act of swallowing' — a colloquial expression for the briefest instant. Even a momentary glance at the uncovered holy things brings death. This is not metaphorical: the Kohathites' restriction is total and absolute. The principle that drives the entire chapter is that YHWH's holiness is not safe for the unconsecrated, regardless of how briefly the exposure occurs.
4.21: YHWH addresses Moses a third time (v21-28) for the Gershonite assignments. The structural repetition — divine address + census description + work assignment + supervisory assignment — establishes the organizational pattern for all three Levitical clans. The Gershonites carry the curtains and coverings (the textile elements of the tabernacle), a lighter but substantial load given the size of the curtain system (Exod 26).
4.22: nasa et rosh beney Gershon: The Gershonites are the second clan counted. They are descendants of Gershon, Levi's firstborn (Gen 46:11), yet they receive a secondary assignment — not the most holy objects but the curtains and coverings. Birth order does not determine holiness rank in Israel; YHWH's assignment determines function. The Kohathites, though second-born (through Kohath, Levi's second son), carry the holiest objects.
4.23: The Gershonites also serve from age 30-50. The same tsava (service/host) language used in v3 appears here — tzava also describes the military host, and both military service and sanctuary service share the term. The Levites are YHWH's army, organized, age-stratified, and commanded, though their weapons are curtains and poles.
4.24: zot avodat mishpechot haGershuni la'avod ulmassa: 'service and carrying.' The two words 'avodah (service/work) and massa (burden/load to be carried) define the Gershonites' dual role: they perform the service of caring for the fabric elements and bear the physical load of carrying them. The distinction between 'avodah and massa will recur as a legal category in later Levitical regulation.
4.25: The Gershonites carry: the curtains of the tabernacle (yeri'ot hamishkan), the tent of meeting with its covering, the covering of tachash leather over it, the screen for the door of the tent, the courtyard hangings. This is the largest textile load — multiple layers of large curtains that form the walls, roof, and courtyard enclosure of the tabernacle complex. Without the Gershonite load, the tabernacle cannot be erected at the next campsite.
4.26: The courtyard screen (masak sha'ar hechatser) and the cords (meytarim) that secure the courtyard — the Gershonites carry everything woven or fabricated for the tabernacle's external structure. The meticulous enumeration of each textile element mirrors the construction specifications of Exodus 26-27, creating a literary correspondence between the construction account and the carrying account.
4.27: al pi Aharon uvanav tihyeh kol avodat beney haGershuni: 'under the direction of Aaron and his sons shall all the work of the Gershonites be done.' The Gershonites are not self-supervising — all their service (carrying, erecting, dismantling) is done under Aaronic direction. This chain of command mirrors the broader structure: YHWH → Moses → Aaron and sons → Levitical clans. The Levites serve the priesthood; the priesthood serves YHWH.
4.28: vetachat yad Ithamar ben Aharon hakohen: Ithamar, Aaron's fourth and youngest son, supervises the Gershonites (as he also supervises the Merarites in v33). The assignment of the two younger Aaron's sons (Eleazar to Kohath, Ithamar to Gershon and Merarite) distributes supervisory responsibility across the priestly family. After Nadab and Abihu's deaths (Lev 10), only Eleazar and Ithamar remain to carry the priestly line.
4.29: The Merarites receive the third census and assignment. They carry the structural-load elements: frames (qershim), crossbars (berichim), pillars ('ammudim), bases ('adenim), pegs (yetedot), ropes (meytarim). The Merarite load is the heaviest in physical weight — the bronze and acacia-wood frame elements — though it lacks the holiness-danger of the Kohathite load. Their census result (3,200, v44) is the largest of the three clans, appropriate given their heavier physical burden.
4.30: The Merarites begin their service at the same 30-50 year range. The repetition of the age-range across all three clans (v3, v23, v30) establishes it as the universal standard for full Levitical service, not a clan-specific rule. The age range may reflect the physical demands of tabernacle carrying — thirty is mature enough for responsibility; fifty is when the physical strength for heavy carrying begins to decline.
4.31: vezeh mishmeret massa'am: 'this is what they are responsible to carry as their entire service.' The Merarites are given itemized responsibility for the structural elements: the frames (qershim), the crossbars (berichim), the pillars and their bases for the tabernacle and courtyard. The level of specification — every peg, every rope — establishes individual accountability for each component. The tabernacle is a complex structure and each piece has a guardian.
4.32: The pegs (yetedot) and ropes (meytarim) — the smallest and least glamorous components — are also itemized and assigned to the Merarites. The thoroughness of the inventory extends to what might seem like insignificant hardware. No component of the tabernacle is beneath the level of named responsibility. The principle: everything that enables YHWH's dwelling to stand is important, regardless of its visibility or perceived significance.
4.33: vetachat yad Ithamar ben Aharon hakohen: Ithamar supervises both Gershon and Merarite — the two clans that carry the less-holy but still essential structural and textile elements. Eleazar supervises the Kohathites who carry the most holy objects. The distribution of oversight reflects the hierarchy of holiness: the higher the holiness-risk, the higher the supervisory rank required.
4.34: The formal census results begin (v34-49). Moses, Aaron, and the leaders count each clan systematically. The verb pakad (muster/number/assign) appears repeatedly throughout Numbers 4 — it carries both the sense of counting and of appointing/assigning. The census is not merely a headcount but a commissioning: each counted Levite is assigned a specific burden and held accountable for it.
4.35: The age qualification formula is restated at the census results: 'all from thirty to fifty years old who qualified to serve.' The repetition between the command (v3) and the census-result (v35) confirms that the command was executed faithfully and completely. This correspondence between divine command and faithful execution is a structural feature of both the Exodus construction narrative and the Numbers census narratives.
4.36: Kohathite count: alpayim sheva me'ot vachamishshim — 2,750. The specific number mirrors the precision of the tabernacle specifications. These are not round estimates but precise records of the working-age population of the Kohathite sub-clan at a specific historical moment (the second year after the Exodus).
4.37: The summary formula: 'These are those enrolled from the Kohathite clans — all who served (kol ha'oved avodah) in the tent of meeting — whom Moses and Aaron enrolled.' The double attestation (Moses and Aaron together) and the reference to YHWH's command through Moses creates a chain of authority: YHWH → Moses → Aaron → census → Kohathites. The Levitical organization is divinely ordered at every level.
4.38: Gershonite census begins. The same formula structure (by clans, ancestral houses, age range) is applied to each sub-clan, creating a meticulous parallel structure. The literary pattern enforces the organizational equality of the three clans in terms of procedural rigor, even though their specific assignments differ in holiness-proximity.
4.39: The qualifying phrase restated for Gershon: 'all from thirty to fifty years old who qualified to work.' The Hebrew qal-haba' litsvo' (everyone who enters to serve, lit. enters to the host) suggests something like 'all who are enrolled into the service-army' — the Levites are YHWH's holy army of sanctuary workers, organized like a military unit.
4.40: Gershonite count: alpayim shesh me'ot usheloshim — 2,630. Slightly less than the Kohathites (2,750), consistent with the typical Kohathite demographic dominance in Numbers' Levitical censuses. The three numbers together (Kohath 2,750 + Gershon 2,630 + Merarite 3,200 = 8,580) will be confirmed in v48.
4.41: Summary for Gershon: same double-attestation formula as v37. The consistent structure across the three clan summaries creates a literary triptych — each clan is treated with identical procedural honor, regardless of the relative holiness of its assigned objects.
4.42: Merarite census introduction. The Merarites, carrying the structural load, are the largest sub-clan (3,200 in v44), suggesting that YHWH's organization of the Levites accounted for the physical demands of each carrying assignment by allocating appropriate workforce.
4.43: The qualifying age range formula restated a third time — its repetition across all three sub-clan censuses is itself a literary statement of thoroughness and order. The God who organized the tabernacle's construction with precise specifications organizes its transportation with the same precision.
4.44: Merarite count: sheloshet alafim umayim — 3,200. The largest of the three sub-clans, carrying the heaviest physical load. The organizational wisdom visible here is that YHWH assigned the most structurally essential (and heaviest) load to the most numerous sub-clan — a practical matching of workforce to workload.
