19.16: vekhol asher yiga al-penei hasadeh bechalalei-cherev o bemet o be'etzem adam o bekever yitma shivat yamim: 'whoever in the open field touches one who has been killed with a sword or who has died naturally, or touches a human bone or a grave, shall be unclean seven days.' The corpse-impurity law extends to the open field: battlefield dead, natural deaths, bones, and graves all convey impurity. Chalal cherev (slain by sword) — battlefield deaths; et etzem adam o kever (human bone or grave). Even touching a grave conveys impurity — this is the background to Jesus's 'whitewashed tombs' accusation (Matt 23:27): Pharisees, who seek to appear righteous externally while internally spreading death, are like the graves that invisibly communicate impurity to those who unknowingly walk over them.
19.17: velakhu latameh meavar hasreifah hachatat venatan alav mayim chayyim el-keli: 'for the unclean they shall take some of the ashes of the burned sin offering and add fresh water to them in a vessel.' Avar hasreifah (from the dust of the burning): the ash of the heifer combined with mayim chayyim (living/fresh water — flowing spring water, not stagnant) creates the purification solution. The paradox continues: inert ash + living water = purifying liquid.
19.18: velakach ezov vetaval bamayim ish tahor vehizah al-ha'ohel ve'al kol-hakeilim ve'al hanefashot asher hayu sham: 'a clean person shall take hyssop and dip it in the water and sprinkle it on the tent and on all the furnishings and on the persons who were there.' Ezov (hyssop) — the sprinkling implement (cf. Exod 12:22 Passover; Ps 51:7: 'purify me with hyssop, and I shall be clean'). Hyssop appears at Passover, in skin disease purification, and here — consistently associated with blood-and-water cleansing. The hyssop reappears at the crucifixion (John 19:29).
19.19: vehizah hatahor al-hatameh bayom hashlishi uvayom hashvi'i vechite'o vayom hashvi'i vecibes begadav verachatz bamayim vetaher ba'arev: 'the clean person shall sprinkle it on the unclean on the third day and on the seventh day. Thus on the seventh day he shall cleanse him, and he shall wash his clothes and bathe himself in water, and at evening he shall be clean.' The purification process: sprinkled day 3 and day 7; washed on day 7; clean at evening. The progression from impure to clean mirrors the creation-week structure, ending at the Sabbath moment (day 7 evening).
19.20: ve'ish asher yitma velo yitchata venikretah ha-nefesh hahi mitkoch hakahal: 'but the man who is unclean and does not cleanse himself, that person shall be cut off from the midst of the assembly.' Venikretah (cut off/karet) — the covenant sanction for refusing purification. The one who refuses the provision for cleansing is cut off; the provision itself is not the problem, but refusing it is fatal. The red heifer's ash is the provision YHWH has made; ignoring it costs the covenant relationship.
19.21: vetahayetah lahem lechukat olam umaze mei haniddah yechabes begadav vehanogea bemei haniddah yitma ad-ha'arev: 'and it shall be a statute forever for them. The one who sprinkles the water for impurity shall wash his clothes, and the one who touches the water for impurity shall be unclean until the evening.' Even the sprinkler incurs temporary impurity. The eternal statute (chukat olam) governs this: the mechanism that cleanses cannot itself be touched without cost. The paradox is permanent — built into creation-order.
19.22: vekhol asher yiga bo hatameh yitma vehanefesh hanogaat tetama ad-ha'arev: 'whatever the unclean person touches shall be unclean, and whoever touches it shall be unclean until the evening.' The impurity transfer principle: the unclean communicates impurity by touch; the contamination extends through a single contact chain. This is why the red heifer law is necessary: without systematic purification, impurity could spread indefinitely through a community that regularly encounters death.

20.1: vayavo'u vnei-Yisrael kol-ha'edah midbar-Tzin bachodesh harishon vayeshev ha'am beKadesh vatamot sham Miriam vatikkaVer sham: 'the people of Israel came into the wilderness of Zin in the first month, and the people stayed in Kadesh. And Miriam died there and was buried there.' Miriam's death is reported in a single verse with no mourning record — in sharp contrast to Aaron's later death (vv28-29) and Moses's anticipated death (Deut 34). Miriam was the first of the three Exodus siblings to die, and Numbers places her death immediately before the water-crisis (vv2-13). Kadesh = holy/sacred; the place named for holiness is the site of both death and the sin at the waters.
20.2: velo-hayah mayim la'edah vayikahalu al-Moshe ve'al-Aharon: 'and there was no water for the congregation. And they assembled themselves against Moses and against Aaron.' Vayikahalu (assembled against) — from kahal (assembly), but used here with the preposition al (against): the assembly forms itself in opposition. The same pattern as Korah's rebellion (16:3, 42). The repetition of wilderness murmuring at water-shortage (cf. Exod 17:1-7 at Rephidim/Massah-Meribah) signals a pattern of unbelief across the generation.
20.3: vayarev ha'am im-Moshe vayomeru lemor: 'and the people quarreled with Moses and said.' Vayarev from rib (contend/quarrel) — the word that gives Meribah (Merivah) its name (v13). The people's specific lament: 'if only we had died when our brothers died before YHWH' — they prefer the deaths of Korah's rebellion as their own fate over the present thirst. An extraordinary statement of despair.
20.4: velama heve'tem et-kehal YHWH el-hamidbar hazeh lamut sham anachnu uve'irenu: 'why have you brought the assembly of YHWH into this wilderness to die here, both we and our livestock?' Kehal YHWH (the assembly of YHWH) — the people identify themselves as YHWH's assembly even as they rebel. The complaint mirrors Exodus 17 almost word for word, showing the wilderness generation's cyclical unbelief despite the earlier Rephidim miracle.
20.5: velama he'eletunu mimitzrayim lavi'enu el-hamakom hara hazeh: 'why have you made us come up out of Egypt to bring us to this evil place?' The rhetorical question that appears repeatedly in wilderness complaints: 'why Egypt?' The questioners define Egypt as good and the wilderness as evil — inverting the Exodus's entire theological framework. Makom hara hazeh (this evil place) — their evaluation of YHWH's chosen wilderness road.
20.6: vayavo Moshe ve-Aharon miphney hakahal el-petach ohel-mo'ed vayiplu al-pneihem: 'Moses and Aaron went from the presence of the assembly to the entrance of the tent of meeting and fell on their faces.' The response to crisis: prostration before the tabernacle entrance. YHWH's glory (kevod YHWH) appears to them — as in the post-Korah plague intercession (17:10, Hebrew). The pattern: crisis → Moses/Aaron to the tent entrance → divine appearance.
20.7: vayedaber YHWH el-Moshe lemor: YHWH speaks. The divine response to the water crisis is immediate. The instruction that follows is clear and specific — distinguishing this episode from Rephidim (Exod 17) where Moses was told to strike the rock.
20.8: kach et-hamateh vehakel et-ha'edah atah ve-Aharon achicha vedibertem el-hassela le'eineihem venatan memaiv: 'take the staff, and assemble the congregation, you and Aaron your brother, and tell [diber] the rock before their eyes to yield its water.' Two commands: take the staff and speak to the rock (daber, not naka = strike). The rock of Rephidim had to be struck (Exod 17:6); this rock is to be addressed by word. The shift from striking to speaking reflects matured covenant communication — the word is sufficient.
20.9: vayikach Moshe et-hamateh milifney YHWH ka'asher tzivahu: 'Moses took the staff from before YHWH, as he commanded him.' Moses obeys the first part of the command — he takes the staff. The staff (matteh) from before YHWH is likely Aaron's budding rod (17:10: 'put back the staff of Aaron before the testimony, to be kept as a sign'). Moses carries the staff of confirmed divine authority into the moment of crisis.
20.10: vayakehilu Moshe ve-Aharon et-hakahal el-penei hassela vayomer lahem shim'u-na hamoriyim: 'Moses and Aaron gathered the assembly together before the rock, and he said to them: Hear now, you rebels.' Hamoriyim — 'rebels' (from marah, to rebel/be bitter). This is Moses addressing the people — but the anger is his, not YHWH's command. The tone has shifted from faithful intercession (v6: falling on his face) to confrontational accusation.
20.11: vayarem Moshe et-yado vayak et-hassela bemattehu pa'amayim vayetze mayim rabbim vatesh ha'edah uve'ireyhem: 'Moses lifted up his hand and struck the rock with his staff twice, and water came out abundantly, and the congregation drank, and their livestock.' Moses strikes the rock — not once but twice. YHWH's command was to speak; Moses strikes. Water comes out abundantly (mayim rabbim) — the miracle occurs regardless of Moses's disobedience, but the sin has been committed. The abundance of water makes the disobedience more poignant: YHWH provides despite the failure of his servant.
20.12: vayomer YHWH el-Moshe ve'el-Aharon yaan lo-he'emantem bi lehaqdisheni le'einei vnei Yisrael lachen lo tavi'u et-hakahal hazeh el-ha'aretz asher natati lahem: 'YHWH said to Moses and Aaron: Because you did not believe in me, to uphold me as holy in the eyes of the people of Israel, therefore you shall not bring this assembly into the land that I have given them.' The sentence is severe and its grounds are specific: lo he'emantem bi (you did not believe/trust in me) + lehaqdisheni (to sanctify me/make me holy before them). The sin was not merely procedural disobedience but a failure of trust and a failure to demonstrate YHWH's holiness. He'emin (believe/trust) — the same root as amen; kadesh (sanctify) — the same root as holiness/sanctuary. The two failures are theological, not merely ritual.
20.13: hemah mei merivah asher-ravu vnei-Yisrael et-YHWH vayikadesh bam: 'these are the waters of Meribah, where the people of Israel quarreled with YHWH, and through them he showed himself holy.' Mei Merivah (waters of Meribah/quarreling): the place-name encodes Israel's sin. Vayikadesh bam: 'he showed himself holy through them' — despite and through the failure, YHWH's holiness was demonstrated. The holy name was not ultimately profaned; Moses's failure became the very occasion for YHWH's holiness to be visible.
20.14: vayishlach Moshe mal'achim miKadesh el-melech Edom: 'Moses sent messengers from Kadesh to the king of Edom.' Edom is Israel's brother-nation — Esau's descendants. The message opens with koh amar achyicha Yisrael (thus says your brother Israel): covenant kinship is invoked. The request for passage through the derech hamelech (King's Highway) — the major north-south trade route east of the Jordan.
20.15: veyede'u et asher asu-lanu mitzrayim: 'you know all the hardship that has come upon us, how our fathers went down to Egypt.' Moses's diplomatic speech invokes shared historical memory — the Egyptian sojourn affects both Israel and Edom as descendants of Jacob/Esau. The appeal is fraternal.
20.16: vayishma YHWH bekoleinu vayishlach malach vayotzi'enu mimitzrayim: 'YHWH heard our voice and sent an angel and brought us out of Egypt.' The Exodus summary in diplomatic language: YHWH heard, sent an angel (malach), brought out. The malach of the Exodus is a recurring figure (Exod 14:19; 23:20; 33:2) whose identity is deeply debated — the Angel of YHWH who is identified with YHWH himself.
20.17: na ne'evrah be'artzecha lo na'avor besadeh uvekerem velo nishteh mei be'er derech hamelech nelech lo nitteh yamin usmol: 'please let us pass through your land. We will not pass through field or vineyard, or drink water from a well. We will go along the King's Highway. We will not turn aside to the right hand or to the left.' Derech hamelech (King's Highway) — the major international route; Israel promises to stay on it, taking nothing. The diplomatic protocol is correct; the request is reasonable.
20.18: vayomer elav Edom lo ta'avor bi pen-becharev etze likratecha: 'but Edom said to him: You shall not pass through, lest I come out with the sword against you.' Edom refuses. The fraternal appeal fails. The hostility between Israel and Edom — rooted in the Jacob/Esau rivalry (Gen 27) — persists through the wilderness period. The prophets (Obadiah; Isa 34; Amos 1:11) later pronounce judgment on Edom's persistent hostility to Jacob's descendants.
20.19: vayomru elav bnei Yisrael bamesila na'aleh ve'im-memeiv nishteh ani uve'iruy venatati michram vrak ein-davar beragli e'evorah: Israel offers to pay for any water drunk and guarantees it is only a matter of passing through on foot. The repeated protest of harmless passage — beragli e'evorah (on foot I will pass through) — emphasizes Israel's peaceful intent.
20.20: vayomer lo ta'avor vayetze Edom likrato be'am kaved uved-chazak: 'but he said: You shall not pass through. And Edom came out against them with a large army and with a strong force.' Am kaved uved chazak (heavy people and strong hand) — military terms. Israel turns aside. The road through Edom is blocked; Israel will have to go around, adding distance and hardship to the wilderness journey.
20.21: vayema'en Edom netet et-Yisrael avor bigvulo vayyet Yisrael me'alav: 'Edom refused to give Israel passage through his territory, so Israel turned away from him.' Vayma'en (refused) — Edom's repeated refusal. Israel turns away (vayyet) — literally 'veered away.' The word anticipates the later wilderness journey narrative where veering away from YHWH's path is a constant temptation.
20.22: vayisu miKadesh vayavo'u vnei Yisrael kol-ha'edah hor hahar: 'and they journeyed from Kadesh, and the people of Israel, the whole congregation, came to Mount Hor.' Hor hahar (Mount Hor) — a place name meaning literally 'the mountain of the mountain' or 'peak mountain,' location uncertain (traditionally identified with Jabal Harun near Petra). The next major event is Aaron's death.
20.23: vayomer YHWH el-Moshe ve'el-Aharon behor hahar al-gevul eretz-Edom lemor: YHWH speaks at the Edom border. The connection between Edom's refusal and Aaron's death is temporal: the rejected passage and the dying of the old generation continue in sequence.
20.24: ye'asef Aharon el-amav ki lo yavo el-ha'aretz asher natati livnei Yisrael yaan asher meritem et-pi lemei merivah: 'Aaron shall be gathered to his people, for he shall not enter the land that I have given to the people of Israel, because you rebelled against my command at the waters of Meribah.' Ye'asef (shall be gathered) — the nif'al passive: Aaron is gathered; his death is a being-taken. El-amav (to his people): the patriarchal death formula (cf. Gen 25:8 Abraham; 35:29 Isaac) — covenant belonging continues beyond death. The reason for Aaron's death outside the land is traced explicitly to Merivah (asher meritem et-pi: you rebelled against my mouth/command).
20.25: kach et-Aharon ve'et-Eleazar beno veha'el otam hor hahar: 'take Aaron and Eleazar his son and bring them up to Mount Hor.' The three-generation ascent: Moses leads Aaron and Eleazar up the mountain. The father-son priestly succession is embedded in the death narrative.
20.26: ufashat et-Aharon et-begadav vehalbashet otam et-Eleazar beno veAharon ye'asef umet sham: 'and strip Aaron of his garments and put them on Eleazar his son. And Aaron shall be gathered to his people and shall die there.' The transfer of the high-priestly garments from Aaron to Eleazar is the transfer of the priesthood itself. Ufashat... vehalbashet (strip... and put on): the investiture of Eleazar is simultaneously the divestiture of Aaron. The high-priestly office cannot die; it passes from father to son in the moment of death. Heb 7:23-24: the Levitical priests 'were prevented by death from continuing in office; but he [Christ] holds his priesthood permanently, because he continues forever.'
20.27: vaya'as Moshe ka'asher tzivah YHWH vaya'alu el-hor hahar le'einei kol-ha'edah: 'Moses did as YHWH commanded. They went up to Mount Hor in the sight of all the congregation.' Le'einei kol-ha'edah (in the sight of all the congregation) — the succession is public, visible to the whole community. The priestly transition is not private or secret; the entire assembly witnesses Aaron's death and Eleazar's installation.
20.28: vayifashet Moshe et-Aharon et-begadav vayalbes otam et-Eleazar beno vayamat Aharon sham bero'sh hahar: 'Moses stripped Aaron of his garments and put them on Eleazar his son. And Aaron died there on the top of the mountain.' Aaron dies clothed in the priestly absence of his garments — having given them to the next generation. The death of Aaron at the mountain top: naked of his office, yet within it. He dies as the priestly generation passes; the office lives in Eleazar.
20.29: vayir'u kol-ha'edah ki gava Aharon vayivku et-Aharon shloshim yom kol beit Yisrael: 'when all the congregation saw that Aaron had breathed his last, all the house of Israel wept for Aaron thirty days.' Gava (breathed his last/expired) — the dying verb; vayivku (wept). Thirty days of mourning — the same as Moses will receive (Deut 34:8). The full-community mourning for Aaron stands in sharp contrast to the one-sentence burial of Miriam (v1). Aaron's death is communally mourned; he was the beloved high priest who stood between the living and the dead (16:48).

25.1: wayyaḥel haˁam liznot (וַיָּחֶל הָעָם לִזְנוֹת) — 'the people began to commit fornication.' Ḥalal in hiphil = to begin, but also to profane/defile. The form carries both: to begin is to defile. Liznot (from zanah = to fornicate, play the harlot) is the vocabulary of spiritual and physical unfaithfulness throughout the prophets (Ezek 16, 23; Hos 1-3). Shittim (שִׁטִּים, acacia trees) — Israel's final campsite before Canaan; the first failure happens at the threshold of the promise.
25.2: wayyiqreˀu laˁam leziḇḥey ˀeloheyhen — the Moabite women invited Israel to the sacrifices of their gods. Cultic meals bind worshippers to the deity they honor: eating at a god's table creates allegiance. Paul uses this principle in 1 Cor 10:18-21: those who eat at the table of demons share with demons. The Baal Peor meal is the anti-type of the Lord's Supper — communion with what is not God.
25.3: wayyiṣamed Yisraˀel levaˁal Peˁor (וַיִּצָּמֶד יִשְׂרָאֵל לְבַעַל פְּעוֹר) — Israel yoked itself to Baal Peor. Ṣamed = to yoke, attach (as animals in a team). The metaphor is deliberate: Israel entered a working partnership with a foreign god — a yoking that is the opposite of the exclusive covenant. Paul cites this incident directly: 'We must not indulge in sexual immorality as some of them did, and twenty-three thousand fell in a single day' (1 Cor 10:8).
25.4: hôqaˁ ˀotam lYHWH (הוֹקַע אוֹתָם לַיהוָה) — 'hang/expose them to the LORD.' Yaqaˁ = to expose, impale, dislocate; a public execution that removes the curse from the community. The public hanging before YHWH in the sun is covenantal procedure: the community dissociates itself from the guilty. Liyhwh — the execution is done before/for YHWH, not merely as punishment but as religious repair.
25.5: ṣimdu levaˁal Peˁor (צְמוּדִים לְבַעַל פְּעוֹר) — yoked to Baal Peor. The hiphil of the previous verse's niphal: the leaders are to put to death those who yoked themselves. The double use of ṣamed reinforces the yoking metaphor: the covenant break is described with agricultural language — they have put on the wrong yoke. Jesus offers his own easy yoke (Matt 11:29-30) in contrast to yoking to false gods.
25.6: An Israelite man brings a Midianite woman leˀeyney Mosheh weleˀeyney kol-ˁedat (לְעֵינֵי מֹשֶׁה וּלְעֵינֵי כָּל-עֲדַת) — before the eyes of Moses and the whole congregation. The brazen public defiance makes the act a theological provocation, not merely a personal sin. The people were weeping at the tent entrance (boke = crying in mourning, perhaps already aware of the plague); the man acts in their sight while they weep.
25.7: Pinḥas ben-Elˁazar ben-Aharon hakohen — Phinehas son of Eleazar son of Aaron. The full genealogy establishes priestly credentials: third-generation Aaronide. wayiqom miqerev haˁedah (וַיָּקָם מִקֶּרֶב הָעֵדָה) — he rose from the midst of the congregation. His is an individual act against communal paralysis; the whole congregation was weeping while one man acted.
25.8: wayyidqor ˀet-sheneyhem (וַיִּדְקֹר אֶת-שְׁנֵיהֶם) — 'he pierced/stabbed through both of them.' Daqar = to pierce, run through with a weapon. The same verb appears in Zech 12:10 ('they will look on me, the one they have pierced') — the word for the eschatological mourning over the one who was pierced is the same as Phinehas' act. Through Phinehas the plague stopped (v.8b); through the pierced one the divine wrath is ultimately exhausted.
25.9: wayyihyu hammetim bammagepha (וַיִּהְיוּ הַמֵּתִים בַּמַּגֵּפָה) — 'those who died in the plague were twenty-four thousand.' This is the largest single plague death toll in the wilderness narrative. Paul in 1 Cor 10:8 gives the number as twenty-three thousand (in one day) — a near-parallel; the difference may reflect counting conventions (per day vs. total) or a different textual tradition.
25.10: YHWH speaks to Moses. The divine speech introduces the theological interpretation of Phinehas' act — his action precedes God's verbal affirmation. In the narrative, physical action stops the plague; God's word follows to explain, reward, and consecrate what zeal accomplished. The sequence matters: zeal acted first; verbal covenant followed.
25.11: qinˀ ˀet-qinˀati (קִנֵּא אֶת-קִנְאָתִי) — 'he was zealous with my zeal/jealousy.' A figura etymologica: he was jealous with my jealousy. Qinˀah = jealousy, zeal — both the passionate desire for exclusive allegiance and the burning wrath when that allegiance is violated. YHWH calls Israel's exclusive devotion with the vocabulary of a jealous spouse (Exod 20:5, ˀel qannaˀ = jealous God). Phinehas' zeal is a human participation in divine jealousy — the covenant's passion expressed through a covenant member.
25.12: brit shalom (בְּרִית שָׁלוֹם) — covenant of peace. The same phrase used for the eschatological covenant in Isa 54:10 ('my covenant of peace will not be removed') and Ezek 34:25 ('I will make a covenant of peace with them'). Phinehas receives the covenant vocabulary that belongs to the messianic age. His zeal that stopped the plague is a type of the atonement that makes peace — shalom as the result of wrath absorbed.
25.13: brit kehunnat ˁolam (בְּרִית כְּהֻנַּת עוֹלָם) — covenant of everlasting priesthood. The Phinehas covenant guarantees his priestly line. kiper ˁal-bney Yisraˀel (כִּפֶּר עַל-בְּנֵי יִשְׂרָאֵל) — he made atonement for Israel. The atonement vocabulary (kipper, ˁalah) applies to Phinehas' action: his spear-thrust was an act of atonement. This is unusual: kipper typically involves blood rituals at the altar. Here the priestly-zealot's direct action stops divine wrath and accomplishes what the altar rites accomplish.
25.14: The Israelite man is identified: Zimri ben-Salu nˀsiˀ bet-ˀav laShimˁoni — Zimri son of Salu, a leader of Simeon. The Simeonite detail resonates: Simeon as a tribe was severely reduced after Baal Peor (ch 26 records only 22,200 Simeonites vs. 59,300 in the first census). The leader of the tribe that suffered most from the plague is the man who provoked the plague at its climax.
25.15: The Midianite woman: Kozbi bat-Tsur (כָּזְבִּי בַת-צוּר) — Cozbi daughter of Zur. Kozbi from kazav = to lie, deceive; Cozbi = 'my lie' or 'deceiver.' The woman whose name means deception is the vector of the Baal Peor apostasy. Zur is a Midianite leader — her status makes the public act more provocative: this is not a casual encounter but an alliance-through-marriage attempt.
25.16: YHWH's speech to Moses: tsaror ˀet-hamMidyanim (צָרוֹר אֶת-הַמִּדְיָנִים) — 'treat the Midianites as enemies.' The root tsarar = to bind, constrain, distress; the niphal means 'enemy' (the one who constrains you). The Midianites used a religious and sexual strategy (through Balaam's counsel, see 31:16) where a military strategy would have failed. Their treachery through enticement rather than war is the model of spiritual deception throughout Scripture.
25.17: nakeh ˀot-haMidyanim (נַכֵּה אֶת-הַמִּדְיָנִים) — 'strike the Midianites.' The command comes immediately after the theological explanation: because they acted deceptively (niklˀu lakem). The judgment is proportionate to the method: they attacked through intimacy; they will be answered with force. The Midianite war (Num 31) executes this command.
25.18: bˀnikhleyhem (בְּנִכְלֵיהֶם, in/through their schemes/treachery) — from nakal = to scheme, act treacherously. The Midianites planned what Balaam could not accomplish through cursing (Num 22-24). The Balaam-Peor connection: Balaam advised Midian to use women as the weapon that curses couldn't provide. The treachery was theological: they used Israel's own sexuality as the mechanism of apostasy.

26.1: ˀaḥarey hammagefa (אַחֲרֵי הַמַּגֵּפָה, after the plague) — the second census is triggered by the plague's end. One generation has been counted (Num 1); a plague has killed the last survivors; now the next generation is counted before entering the land. The census marks the transition: those counted in Num 1 were condemned never to enter; those counted here are the ones who will. The arithmetic of judgment has been completed.
26.2: seˀu ˀet-roˀsh kol-ˁadat (שְׂאוּ אֶת-רֹאשׁ כָּל-עֲדַת) — 'lift the head of all the congregation.' The nasaˀ roˀsh formula for census-taking (also in Num 1:2) = to count, literally 'to lift the head' of each person. The census recognizes each individual as significant enough to be counted; the roˀsh (head) is lifted — honored, acknowledged, registered.
26.3: ˁarvot Moˀav ˁal-Yarden Yˀricho (עַרְבֹת מוֹאָב עַל-יַרְדֵּן יְרִיחוֹ) — plains of Moab beside the Jordan opposite Jericho. This is Israel's final campsite for the rest of Numbers, Deuteronomy, and the threshold of Joshua. Jericho visible across the Jordan is the horizon of promise. Every significant event from here to Joshua 1 happens in this geographic frame — the edge of the land.
26.4: kaˀasher ṣivvah YHWH ˀet-Mosheh — as the LORD commanded Moses. The census repeats the command structure of Num 1. YHWH commands → Moses obeys → the people are numbered. The command drives the narrative, not military need or administrative convenience. The census is an act of obedience, not just organization.
26.5: Reuben's clans: Hˀnochi, haPalluˀi, haHetsroni, haKarmi. Reuven (Reuben) = 'see, a son' (Gen 29:32, Leah's cry at his birth). The firstborn tribe lists its clans in the order of their progenitors. Despite Reuben's loss of the firstborn blessing (Gen 49:3-4), the tribe is counted first — genealogical priority survives moral failure in God's census.
26.6: Reuben's sub-clans continued. The clan system (mishpaḥot) is the social unit between tribe and individual: typically a group of related families sharing a common ancestor and holding land together. The clan names preserve the memory of the ancestors three or four generations removed from Jacob.
26.7: Reuben total: 43,730 — down from 46,500 in the first census (Num 1:21). The reduction of 2,770 men represents the toll of forty years of wilderness mortality and divine judgment on this tribe. Every tribal reduction is a theological accounting.
26.8: The son of Pallu: Eliab. The genealogical notation here serves as a transition to the Dathan-Abiram narrative embedded in the census. The census is not merely a counting of living men; it includes the theological history of the families being counted.
26.9: The sons of Eliab: Nemuel, Dathan, and Abiram — the Dathan and Abiram who 'were prominent in the congregation' (qˀrˀy haˁedah). Qˀruˀey = those summoned/called — the chosen leaders who used their prominence to rebel against Moses and Aaron. The census interpolates the rebellion of Num 16 to explain why the Reubenite line of Eliab records no descendants from Dathan and Abiram.
26.10: The earth swallowed them and Korah when fire devoured the two hundred and fifty. wayihyu lˀnes (וַיִּהְיוּ לְנֵס, they became a sign/warning) — a lasting sign. Nes = sign, banner, pole (the same word as the Nehushtan pole in Num 21:8). The catastrophic judgment becomes a permanent visual warning embedded in tribal genealogy — their absence from the clan list is itself the testimony.
26.11: ubney Qoraḥ loˀ metu (וּבְנֵי-קֹרַח לֹא-מֵתוּ) — 'but the sons of Korah did not die.' The remarkable parenthetical. Korah was swallowed; his followers were consumed; his sons survived. The Psalms superscriptions (Pss 42-49, 84-85, 87-88) are attributed to the sons of Korah — Israel's greatest choirmasters came from the family of the rebellion's leader. God's judgment is precise; his preservation of the innocent in the midst of judgment is equally precise.
26.12: Simeon's clans: Nemuel, Jamin, Jachin, Zerah, Shaul. Shimˁon (Simeon) = 'YHWH has heard' (Gen 29:33). Five clans. In the first census Simeon numbered 59,300 (Num 1:23); the second census (v.14) will record 22,200 — a reduction of 37,100, the largest tribal loss. The Baal Peor catastrophe was primarily a Simeonite disaster (the offending leader was Zimri, a Simeonite chief, 25:14).
26.13: Simeon's sub-clans continued: Zerah and Shaul. Zeraḥ (Zerah) = 'rising/dawn' — the same name appears in Judah's genealogy (Gen 38:30; Num 26:20). The coincidence of clan names across tribes reflects the common ancestral pool from which Israel's tribal system is drawn.
26.14: Simeon total: 22,200 — down 63% from 59,300. This is the most dramatic tribal decline in the census. The tribe's prominence in the Baal Peor apostasy is the theological explanation. Jacob's prophecy (Gen 49:5-7) foretold that Simeon and Levi would be scattered in Israel; the census shows it beginning with Simeon's collapse.
26.15: Gad's clans: Zephon, Haggi, Shuni, Ozni, Eri, Arod, Areli. Gad = 'fortune/good fortune' (Gen 30:11). Seven clan names — suggesting a fuller preservation of Gad's sons in the census. Gad will eventually settle east of the Jordan (Num 32), outside the main land boundaries.
26.16: Gad's sub-clans: Ozni (from Ezbon in Gen 46:16) and Eri. The variation between Gen 46's birth list and the Num 26 census list reflects the generational distance — the census counts the clans (mishpaḥot) descended from the sons, not the sons themselves by this point.
26.17: Gad's sub-clans continued: Arod and Areli. The seven-clan structure of Gad (compared to Simeon's five) suggests proportionally more clan survival through the wilderness generation. The clan count is not merely organizational — it reflects the relative vitality of each tribal family.
26.18: Gad total: 40,500 — down from 45,650 (first census). The tribal arithmetic of loss: 5,150 men fewer. Each number encodes forty years of mortality and divine judgment. The census is simultaneously a counting of the living and an implicit memorial for the dead.
26.19: Judah's sons: Er and Onan — but wayyamot Er waˀOnan bˀˀerets Kˀnaˁan (they died in Canaan). The census embeds the narrative of Gen 38:7-10: Er was wicked; Onan violated the levirate obligation. Their pre-exodus deaths remove two Judahite lines from the census. Judah's tribal continuity runs through Shelah, Pharez (Perez), and Zerah.
26.20: Judah's surviving clans: Shelanites, Pharzites (Perezites), Zerahites. Pharez/Perez (פֶּרֶץ) = 'breakthrough' — named at his birth when he pushed ahead of his twin (Gen 38:29). The Pharzite line is the messianic line: Perez → Hezron → Ram → Amminadab → Nahshon → Salmon → Boaz → Obed → Jesse → David → ... → Jesus (Matt 1:3-16; Luke 3:31-33). The census name Pharzites quietly encodes the entire Davidic line.
26.21: The Pharzites sub-divided: Hezron (Hezronites) and Hamul (Hamulites). Hezron appears in the genealogy of Ruth and Matthew: Gen 46:12; Ruth 4:18; Matt 1:3. The census subdivision of the messianic line into its next generation is a quiet genealogical thread that will lead eventually to Bethlehem.
26.22: Judah total: 76,500 — up from 74,600 (first census). The increase of 1,900 is notable: while most tribes declined, Judah grew. Jacob's blessing (Gen 49:8-12) promised Judah's preeminence; the census documents the beginning of its demographic fulfillment. The royal tribe strengthens as the covenant people move toward the land where their king will rise.
26.23: Issachar's clans: Tola, Puvah, Jashub, Shimron. Yissachar (Issachar) = possibly 'man of wages' or 'he will bring wages' (Gen 30:18). Four clans. Issachar's blessing from Jacob emphasizes strength and labor (Gen 49:14-15); the tribe settled in the fertile Jezreel Valley.
26.24: Issachar sub-clans: Puvah/Pua (Punites) and Jashub (Jashubites). The name Jashub = 'he returns' — from the root shuv, the repentance vocabulary. The clan's name carries the language of return and turning that will become the central vocabulary of the prophets' call to restoration.
26.25: Issachar total: 64,300 — up from 54,400 (first census), an increase of 9,900. The largest proportional increase among the tribes at this point. Issachar's growth during the wilderness period is unexplained by the narrative but documented by the census. YHWH's sovereignty over tribal demographics is not a function of moral performance alone.
26.26: Zebulun's clans: Sered, Elon, Jahleel. Zevulun (Zebulun) = 'dwelling' or 'honor' (Gen 30:20). Three clans. Zebulun's territory will border the Sea of Galilee — the region of Jesus' primary ministry (Isa 9:1; Matt 4:13-16). The census preserves Zebulun's three founding clans for the tribe that will host the Light of the World.
26.27: Zebulun total: 60,500 — down from 57,400 (first census); actually an increase of 3,100. The text in most MSS gives 60,500. Zebulun's modest growth mirrors the overall pattern: the covenant community survives the wilderness with demographic continuity despite the forty-year mortality sentence.
26.28: Joseph's sons: Manasseh and Ephraim. Joseph counted as two tribes (Gen 48:5 — Jacob adopted Joseph's sons as his own). The double portion of Joseph is embedded in the tribal structure: where the other tribes have one entry, Joseph has two. This is the double portion of the firstborn — transferred from Reuben to Joseph (1 Chr 5:1-2) because of Reuben's moral failure.
26.29: Manasseh's clans: liMˀkir yˀlad ˀet-Gilˁad (לְמָכִיר יֻלַּד אֶת-גִּלְעָד) — Machir fathered Gilead. The genealogical embedding: Manasseh → Machir → Gilead → sub-clans. Gilaˁd (Gilead) = 'heap of witness/testimony' — the territory east of the Jordan where part of Manasseh will settle (Num 32). The genealogy maps directly onto the territorial claim.
26.30: Gilead's sub-clans: Iezer (Abiezer), Helek, Asriel, Shechem, Shemida, Hepher. The density of sub-clan names here reflects Manasseh's large population and land-claims. Abiezer will appear again in Judges 6:11 as Gideon's clan; Shechem is the city at the center of the land.
26.31: Gilead's sub-clans continued: Asriel, Shechem, Shemida. Shechem (שְׁכֶם) = 'shoulder/ridge' — both a place name and a clan name. The toponym and the tribal ancestor share their name, showing the deep connection between Manasseh's genealogy and the geography of central Canaan that the tribe will inherit.
26.32: Hepher is named; Zelophehad son of Hepher had no sons, only daughters — Tselopeḥad ben-Ḥefer lˀ-hayu lo banim ki ˀim-banot. The census pauses to register an inheritance anomaly that was adjudicated in Num 27 and Num 36. The daughters of Zelophehad are named in the middle of the census because their right to inherit land is structurally embedded in the tribal count.
26.33: Zelophehad's daughters named: Mahlah, Noah, Hoglah, Milcah, Tirzah. Five daughters who will inherit as a clan in their own right (Num 27:1-11). Tirzah (tirtseh) = 'she is my delight' — also the name of a beautiful city in the northern kingdom (Cant 6:4). The daughters whose legal case established women's inheritance rights are remembered by name in the census.
26.34: Manasseh total: 52,700 — down from 32,200 (first census). Actually up — 32,200 to 52,700 is a dramatic increase of 20,500, the largest absolute increase of any tribe. Manasseh nearly doubled during the wilderness period. The double-portion tribe grows disproportionately, as though YHWH's blessing on Joseph's line is accelerating through the wilderness generation.
26.35: Ephraim's clans: Shuthelah, Becher, Tahan. Ephrayim (Ephraim) = 'fruitful' (Gen 41:52 — Joseph named him 'for God has made me fruitful'). Jacob elevated Ephraim above Manasseh (Gen 48:19-20) and the census reflects this: Ephraim's clans are listed with the Eranites as a major sub-clan.
26.36: Shuthelah's son Eran (Eranites). The layered genealogy: Ephraim → Shuthelah → Eran → Eranites. The depth of the genealogical record through three generations shows which family lines have been most productive and best preserved through the wilderness period.
26.37: Ephraim total: 32,500 — down from 40,500 (first census). A decline of 8,000 despite Jacob's blessing of Ephraim above Manasseh. The younger/greater tribe declined while the older/lesser tribe (Manasseh) grew dramatically. The demographics of blessing resist simple theological arithmetic.
26.38: Benjamin's clans: Bela, Ashbel, Ahiram, Shupham (Shephupham), Hupham. Binyamin (Benjamin) = 'son of the right hand' (or 'son of the south'). Five founding clans. Benjamin is the youngest and smallest of the original tribes — the last son of Jacob and Rachel.
26.39: Benjamin's sub-clans: Ard and Naaman (sons of Bela). The sub-clan structure of Benjamin will become important for the tribe's survival after the near-extermination in Judges 20-21. The detailed genealogical record ensures that even a nearly destroyed tribe can reconstitute itself from its surviving clan structure.
26.40: Benjamin's clans continued: Ard (Ardites) and Naaman (Naamites). Benjamin will produce Saul (Israel's first king) and Paul (apostle to the Gentiles). The census of the tribe that will generate these figures records their founding ancestors without any editorial comment on their future significance.
26.41: Benjamin total: 45,600 — down from 35,400 (first census); actually up 10,200. The significant increase is notable given Benjamin's later near-destruction in the civil war of Judges 20. The census records a moment of tribal growth that the subsequent narrative will threaten to extinguish.
26.42: Dan's clan: Shuham only. Dan = 'he judged' (Gen 30:6). Dan is remarkable for having only a single primary clan — the Shuhamites — with no sub-clans listed. The tribe's genealogical simplicity contrasts with its large population (v.43).
26.43: Dan total: 64,400 — down from 62,700 (first census); actually up 1,700. With a single clan, Dan is the most genealogically concentrated major tribe. Its large population from one founding line suggests either exceptional reproductive success or a clan-consolidation over the wilderness period.
26.44: Asher's clans: Imnah, Ishvi, Beriah. Asher = 'happy/blessed' (Gen 30:13 — Leah named him this). Three primary clans, with Beriah sub-divided. Asher's blessing from Moses (Deut 33:24) promises: 'Blessed above sons is Asher; let him be the favorite of his brothers, and let him dip his foot in oil' — the fertile coastal territory.
26.45: Beriah's sons: Heber and Malchiel. The sub-clan structure of Beriah (Heber → Heberites; Malchiel → Malchielites) preserves two more generations of Asherite genealogy. Heber the Kenite (Judges 4:11) and his wife Jael (who killed Sisera) — while a different family entirely — show that the name Heber appears in the tribal periphery as well.
26.46: Asher's daughter: Sheraḥ (שֶׂרַח). The female name appears in the census as a named exception — a daughter of Asher recorded by name. This is one of the few named women in the census lists. The reason for the exception is unclear: later tradition credits Serah with extraordinary longevity, surviving from the exodus generation to the time of David.
26.47: Asher total: 53,400 — down from 41,500 (first census); actually up 11,900. A significant increase for a tribe in the coastal region. Asher's growth mirrors the coastal fertility that Moses' blessing anticipates.
26.48: Naphtali's clans: Jahzeel, Guni, Jezer, Shillem. Naphtali = 'my wrestling' (Gen 30:8 — Rachel's wrestling with Leah). Four clans. Naphtali's territory will include the western shore of the Sea of Galilee — the setting of much of Jesus' Galilean ministry (Matt 4:13-16 cites Isa 9:1's Naphtali/Zebulun as the region of the Messiah's light).
26.49: Naphtali's sub-clans: Jahzeelites, Gunites, Jezerites, Shillemites. The four-clan structure gives Naphtali a modest but stable genealogical base. Naphtali declined from 53,400 to 45,400 (8,000 fewer) — one of the larger tribal decreases in the second census.
26.50: Naphtali total: 45,400 — down from 53,400 (first census), a loss of 8,000. The decline of the tribe whose land will host the majority of Jesus' public ministry is narrated without theological comment. The census records facts; the theological significance emerges across centuries of subsequent history.
26.51: ˀeleh pˀquday bney Yisraˀel — the grand total: sheish-meˀot ˀelep weˀeḥad ˀalaphim ushˀvah meˀot ushloˀshim — 601,730. Slightly less than the 603,550 of the first census (Num 1:46). The forty years of plague and judgment have taken a net toll of 1,820 men of military age. The covenant community survived the wilderness — diminished but intact.
26.52: YHWH speaks about the land distribution. The second census is not merely administrative; it is the basis for territorial allocation. Every man counted in the census represents a land claim for his household. The census connects counting to inheritance — being numbered is the precondition of receiving the land.
26.53: lˀˀelleh teḥalek haˀarets bˀnaḥalah (לָאֵלֶּה תֵּחָלֵק הָאָרֶץ בְּנַחֲלָה) — to these the land shall be divided as naḥalah (inheritance). The census-to-land connection is explicit. Naḥalah = ancestral inheritance, the land permanently assigned to a family group. The NT applies this language to the heavenly inheritance (1 Pet 1:4; Eph 1:11).
26.54: larav tarbeh naḥalato wˀlammˁat tamˁit naḥalato — larger tribes receive larger portions, smaller tribes smaller ones. The proportionality principle: the land inheritance is sized to the family that will work it. The principle connects demographic vitality to territorial provision — larger covenant communities require larger land-grants. The heavenly inheritance, by contrast, is equally large for all (John 14:2).
26.55: ˀakh bˀgoral yeḥalek ˀet-haˀarets (אַךְ בְּגוֹרָל יֵחָלֵק אֶת-הָאָרֶץ) — 'yet by lot the land shall be divided.' The goral (lot) system introduces divine sovereignty into the distribution: size determines approximate allocation but the specific location within each size-class is determined by lot. The lot is the mechanism of YHWH's sovereign will (Prov 16:33: 'the lot is cast into the lap, but its every decision is from the LORD').
26.56: Distribution by lot applies to both large and small tribes: the lot determines location, the census determines size. The two-variable system prevents both favoritism (by location) and inequality (by population size). The combination of divine sovereignty (lot) and demographic justice (proportional size) models a governance principle where God's will and human realities are both honored.
26.57: The Levites are listed separately. Their clans: Gershon, Kohath, Merari — the same three that organized the transport of the tabernacle (Num 3-4). Levi = 'attached/joined' (Gen 29:34). The Levites' distinct listing outside the main tribal census foreshadows their distinct role: no land inheritance, but cities and tithes throughout all the other tribes' lands.
26.58: Other Levitical families: Libnites, Hebronites, Mahlites, Mushites, Korahites. The Korah here is the Levitical ancestor (not the rebel of Num 16); the families are distinct. The Mushites are the descendants of Moses' father's family (Mushi from Moses' father Amram's line). The Levitical genealogy preserves both the priestly line and its extended family structure.
26.59: shem ˀeshet ˁAmram Yokheved bat-Levi — Amram's wife Jochebed, daughter of Levi, born to Levi in Egypt. Jochebed is the only person in the Torah described as born to Levi — she is the direct daughter of the patriarch. Her birth is noted in the census because she is the mother of Aaron, Moses, and Miriam — the three leaders of the exodus generation who appear in the same parenthetical as this genealogical note.
26.60: Aaron's sons: Nadab, Abihu, Eleazar, Ithamar. The four sons who were ordained in Lev 8-9. Nadab and Abihu offered unauthorized fire (Lev 10:1-2) and died; the priesthood continued through Eleazar and Ithamar, the two survivors whose lines will divide the priestly service throughout Israel's history.
26.61: wayyamot Nadav waˀAvihu bˀhaqˀrivam ˀesh zarah (וַיָּמָת נָדָב וַאֲבִיהוּא בְּהַקְרִיבָם אֵשׁ זָרָה) — Nadab and Abihu died when they offered unauthorized fire. The census re-registers their deaths from Lev 10: the priestly genealogy incorporates the record of priestly failure. The two deaths reduced Aaron's line to Eleazar and Ithamar — the ancestors of the entire subsequent Israelite priesthood.
26.62: Levi total: 23,000 males one month and older — not counted among the main census because ki loˀ hitpaqˀdu bˀtok bney Yisraˀel ki loˀ nittan lahem naḥalah (they were not numbered among Israel because no inheritance was given to them). The Levites' exclusion from land-inheritance is their census identity: they are counted separately, for a different purpose, with different rules. Their inheritance is YHWH himself (Num 18:20).
26.63: Summary header: Moses and Eleazar the priest registered the Israelites in the plains of Moab by the Jordan. The geographic frame recurs: the Jordan plain opposite Jericho is the setting of the final preparation. Eleazar, not Aaron (who died in ch 20), is now the high priest — the generation of leadership has turned.
26.64: ubˀˀeleh loˀ-haya ˀish meˀpˀqudy Mosheh wˀˀAharon (וּבְאֵלֶּה לֹא-הָיָה אִישׁ מִפְּקוּדֵי מֹשֶׁה וְאַהֲרֹן) — 'and not one of these was among those Moses and Aaron registered.' The entire first census cohort is gone. This is the structural verdict: YHWH's judgment was complete. Every man of military age from the Sinai census — 603,550 souls — died in the wilderness. Not one survived except two.
26.65: ki-ˀamar YHWH lahem mot yamutu bamidbar (כִּי-אָמַר יְהוָה לָהֶם מוֹת יָמוּתוּ בַּמִּדְבָּר) — 'for YHWH had said of them: They shall surely die in the wilderness.' The divine word pronounced in Num 14:29-35 was executed with complete faithfulness: every man of that generation died. wˀloˀ-notar ˀish mehem ki ˀim-Kalev... wiYˀhoshuˁa — 'not one of them remained except Caleb... and Joshua.' Two survivors from 603,550. The ratio of faithfulness to the entire first generation: two in over six hundred thousand. Caleb and Joshua are the remnant theology in miniature — the few who cling to the promise through the dying generation.

30.1: Moses addresses the tribal heads (rāʾšê hammattôt) rather than the whole congregation — the vow laws concern personal obligations whose enforcement falls within family authority structures. The formal opening zeh haddāḇār ʾăšer ṣiwwâ YHWH — this is the word YHWH commanded — frames the following legislation as direct divine command reported through Moses.
30.2: The foundational vow principle: a man who makes a נֶדֶר (neder) — a positive promise to offer something to YHWH — or a שְׁבוּעָה (šəḇûʿâ) — an oath binding himself — must not yəḥallel dəḇārô — not profane/defile his word. The verb חָלַל (ḥālal) in the Piel means to treat as common what is sacred: one's spoken word to YHWH has a sacral quality that makes breaking it a form of profanation. The positive command: ʾăšer yēṣēʾ mippîw yaʿăśeh — whatever proceeds from his mouth he shall do.
30.3: The law pivots to women, introducing three distinct legal categories based on household status. First: a woman in her father's house (bêt ʾāḇîhā) in her youth (binəʿûrêhā). The phrase nāʾ ʾissār ʿal-nap̱šāh — she binds a pledge upon her soul — uses the distinctive vocabulary of this chapter: אִסָּר (ʾissār) from ʾāsar (to bind), denoting a self-binding verbal commitment. The neder is a positive promise; the ʾissār is a self-denial/restriction pledge.
30.4: If her father hears (wəšāmaʿ ʾāḇîhā) and says nothing (weheḥĕrîš lāh) — the legal silence that constitutes tacit approval — all her vows and pledges stand. הֶחֱרִישׁ (heḥĕrîš) is the technical term for legally meaningful silence throughout this chapter (vv4,5,7,8,12,14); it is not mere inaction but a defined legal choice between disallowance and ratification. Silence = ratification.
30.5: If her father hēnîʾ ʾôtāh — disallows/withholds approval from her — on the day he hears, none of her vows stand. The verb נוּא (nûʾ) — to disallow, restrain, refuse — appears primarily in this chapter (vv5,8,9,12 etc.) for the paternal/spousal veto. The reason YHWH gives for the release from the vow: wəYHWH yislaḥ lāh — YHWH will forgive her. The failed vow is not her fault; the one in authority who annulled it bears responsibility.
30.6: Second category: a woman who makes a vow or a rash/hasty pledge (miḇṭāʾ śəp̄ātêhā) — the phrase מִבְטָא שְׂפָתַיִם (miḇṭāʾ śəp̄ātayim) literally means the utterance of lips (from bāṭaʾ, to speak hastily); this suggests impetuous vows made without full deliberation — and then marries (ûl·ʾîš tihyeh). The transition of household authority from father to husband requires a fresh legal rule.
30.7: If her husband hears and says nothing on the day he hears, her vows stand and her binding pledges hold. The same silence-as-ratification principle transfers from the paternal to the spousal context. The consistent structure across both contexts establishes the legal principle: the authority figure's timely silence ratifies; timely speech can annul; belated annulment is too late.
30.8: If her husband disallows (yānîʾ ʾôtāh) on the day he hears, he annuls the vow. The verse includes both the vow (neder) and the rash utterance of her lips (miḇṭāʾ śəp̄ātêhā). The husband's authority to annul on the day of hearing — no later — preserves the woman from vow obligations she has already incurred while preventing retrospective manipulation of vow law.
30.9: Third category: the widow (ʾalmānâ) and the divorced woman (gərûšâ). Their vows stand unconditionally — no paternal or spousal authority exists to annul them. kol-ʾăšer ʾāsərâ ʿal-nap̱šāh yāqûm ʿālêhā — all that she bound upon herself shall stand against her. The language yāqûm ʿālêhā — it stands against her — emphasizes that she alone bears the full weight of her vow commitments. Full legal autonomy comes with full vow responsibility.
30.10: The married-while-making-vow scenario elaborates: a vow or oath made while in her husband's house (bêt ʾîšāh). The same legal structure applies — husband may ratify by silence or annul by timely speech — extended to cover commitments made during the marriage rather than before it.
30.11: If he heard and said nothing and did not disallow her (lōʾ hēnîʾ ʾôtāh), all her vows and pledges stand. The doubling — silence plus non-disallowance — reinforces that the husband must actively exercise his veto on the day of hearing; failure to do so is legally binding ratification, not a recoverable omission.
30.12: If he fully annulled them (hāpēr yāp̄ēr ʾôtām) on the day of hearing — the infinitive absolute emphasizes complete annulment — then whatever she spoke is null. The annulled obligation is specifically categorized as things concerning the soul/person (kol-môṣāʾ śəp̄ātêhā lənādrêhā ûlə ʾissər nap̱šāh); YHWH forgives the unfulfilled vow.
30.13: The summary principle: every vow (kol-neder) and every oath of self-denial (kol-šəḇûʿat ʾissār lənannôt nāp̱eš) — her husband may confirm (yəqîmennû) or annul (yəp̄ērennû). The verb קוּם (qûm) in the Hiphil = to establish, uphold; פָּרַר (pārar) = to break, annul. The binary is absolute: confirmation or annulment, no middle option.
30.14: The silence-ratification principle reaches its precise legal statement: if the husband says nothing day after day (min-yôm ʾel-yôm), he confirms all her vows by the very act of continued silence — he has ratified them. The phrase heqîm ʾôtām kî-heḥĕrîš lāh — he confirmed them because he was silent to her — establishes silence over time as cumulative ratification, creating legal commitment through inaction.
30.15: The critical limiting case: if the husband annuls the vows after hearing them (ʾaḥărê šomʿô), he bears her iniquity (wənāśāʾ ʾet-ʿăwōnāh). The verb נָשָׂא עָוֹן (nāśāʾ ʿāwōn) — to bear/carry iniquity — is the same phrase used for substitutionary bearing of guilt throughout the Torah (Lev 5:1,17; 10:17). Belated annulment transfers moral culpability from the woman (who acted in good faith) to the husband (who delayed improperly).
30.16: The concluding rubric: ʾēlleh haḥuqqîm ʾăšer ṣiwwâ YHWH ʾet-Moshe — these are the statutes YHWH commanded Moses. The scope stated — between a man and his wife, and between a father and his daughter in her youth — precisely delimits the law's application to the two household-authority relationships addressed. The rubric marks the end of the vow-law unit as a discrete legislative section.

31.1: A direct divine speech unit commanding war against Midian. The double speech formula (waydabber YHWH ʾel-Moshe lēmôr) opens a new major section. Midian was the site of the Baal Peor apostasy (Num 25), where Midianite women seduced Israel into idolatry at Balaam's instigation (v16 below), resulting in 24,000 deaths from divine plague.
31.2: nəqōm niqmat bənê yiśrāʾēl mēʾet hammiḏyānîm — take full vengeance for Israel on the Midianites. The cognate accusative nqm nqmh — take vengeance of vengeance — intensifies the certainty and completeness of the required action. This is divine vengeance executed through Israel, not personal vendetta. The timing note: afterward Moses will be gathered to his people (tiʾāsēp̄ ʾel-ʿammekā) — this campaign is Moses's final act before death (Deut 34).
31.3: Moses arms men from the people for war: hēḥālēṣû miṭṭikkem ʾănāšîm lassāḇāʾ — draw out men from among you for the host. The verb חָלַץ (ḥālaṣ) — to draw out, equip — connotes selection from the broader community for a specific military purpose. The goal is explicitly stated: laʿăśôt niqmat YHWH — to execute the vengeance of YHWH — YHWH owns the vengeance, Israel are the instruments.
31.4: One thousand from each tribe (ʾelep̄ lammaṭṭeh ʾelep̄ lammaṭṭeh) — the doubling of the phrase emphasizes the precise proportional levy. The number twelve thousand soldiers (v5) reflects the standard holy-war muster pattern of the tribes contributing equally. The small number — compared to the census figures of 600,000+ — signals this is a symbolic-representative force rather than a total mobilization.
31.5: wayyimmāsərû mēʾalp̄ê yiśrāʾēl ʾelep̄ lammaṭṭeh — one thousand per tribe were drafted. The verb מָסַר (māsar) — to hand over, deliver — has the connotation of conscription, transferring individuals from community service to military command. The total: twelve thousand armed troops (ḥălûṣê ṣāḇāʾ — equipped for the host).
31.6: Phinehas son of Eleazar the priest accompanies the army with the holy vessels (kəlê haqqōḏeš) and the signal trumpets (waḥăṣōṣərôt hamtərûʿâ). Phinehas is the priest who stopped the Baal Peor plague by executing the Israelite man and Midianite woman (Num 25:7-8,11). His presence frames this campaign as the completion of the covenant crisis he interrupted.
31.7: wayyiṣbəʾû ʿal-miḏyān kaʾăšer ṣiwwâ YHWH ʾet-Moshe — they waged war against Midian as YHWH commanded Moses. The war is conducted in explicit obedience to divine command; every action is measured against this standard. wayyahargû kol-zākār — they killed every male. The ḥērem (devoted-destruction) principle applies here even without the word being used: total destruction of combatants.
31.8: The five Midianite kings are named: Evi, Rekem, Zur, Hur, and Reba — the same five named in Josh 13:21 as vassal kings under Sihon of the Amorites, placing Midian in a tributary relationship with the Transjordanian kings already defeated. בִּלְעָם בֶּן-בְּעוֹר (Bilʿam ben Bəʿôr) — Balaam son of Beor — is also killed. The prophet whom Balak hired to curse Israel (Num 22-24) becomes the instrument of Israel's seduction (v16) and meets his end by the sword.
31.9: The women, children, livestock, and goods are taken as spoil. The capture rather than killing of the women becomes the point of Moses's furious confrontation in vv14-18. The spoil enumeration follows the standard sequence of human captives and animals, with property following. The scale of the plunder is precisely tabulated in vv32-47.
31.10: The settled cities (ʿārêhem) and encampments (ṭirôtām) are burned. טִירָה (ṭîrâ) — encampment, unwalled settlement — distinguishes nomadic camp-settlements from fortified cities. Both types of Midianite habitation are destroyed. The thoroughness of the destruction follows the pattern of holy war against devoted peoples (Deut 7:1-5).
31.11: The army gathers all the plunder — ʾet kol-haššālāl wəʾet kol-hammalqôaḥ bāʾāḏām ûḇabbəhēmâ — both among humans and animals. The distinction šālāl (general plunder/spoil) and malqôaḥ (specifically captured livestock/persons) reflects the formal vocabulary of spoil-distribution law that follows in vv25-47.
31.12: The army returns to the camp at the plains of Moab by the Jordan across from Jericho — the final encampment position of the wilderness period. The gathered captives and spoil are brought to Moses and Eleazar for judgment. The three-fold authority — Moses (prophetic), Eleazar (priestly), the congregation (communal) — mirrors the judicial structure of ch15's Sabbath-breaker case.
31.13: Moses, Eleazar, and the leaders go out to meet the returning army. The going-out to meet (wayyēṣəʾû... ligrāʾt) is the standard honor-reception for returning warriors, which makes Moses's subsequent fury (v14) all the more dramatic. He approaches to receive them and instead confronts them.
31.14: wayyiqṣōp̄ Moshe ʿal pəqûḏê heḥāyil — Moses was furious at the officers of the force. קָצַף (qāṣap̄) — to be furious, wrathful — is used for both divine and human anger; the word appears for YHWH's wrath against Israel (Num 1:53; 17:11; 18:5). Moses's rage mirrors the divine anger that required this campaign in the first place. The officers are challenged: why have you kept all the women alive?
31.15: hăḥayyîtem kol-nəqēḇâ — you have let all the females live? The interrogative is rhetorical: Moses already knows the answer. The question establishes the warrant for what follows — the women's survival is presented as a failure to complete the divine mandate, specifically in light of what the women did at Peor.
31.16: The explanatory charge: hēnnâ hāyû liḇnê yiśrāʾēl bidiḇar Bilʿām — these same women were the ones who, following Balaam's counsel, caused Israel to act treacherously against YHWH in the matter of Peor. The phrase biḏbar Bilʿām — according to Balaam's word/counsel — identifies Balaam's strategy: unable to curse Israel directly (Num 22-24), he apparently counseled Moab/Midian to seduce Israel through women (cf. Rev 2:14). The result was the plague in Num 25 (hammageep̱â ʿal-ʿăḏat YHWH).
31.17: The expanded command: kill every male child (kol-zākār battāp̄) and every woman who has known a man sexually (kol-ʾiššâ yōḏaʿat ʾîš). The phrase יָדַע אִישׁ (yāḏaʿ ʾîš) — known a man — is the standard biblical idiom for sexual intercourse (Gen 4:1,17,25). This expanded command applies the ḥērem logic: those who participated in or could continue the seduction-threat to Israel are killed; those who were not party to it are preserved.
31.18: The young girls who have not known a man sexually are kept alive. The distinction between combatant-women (those who participated in the Peor seduction as adults) and non-combatant girls follows a logic similar to Deut 20:13-14, which distinguishes between male combatants (killed) and women and children (preserved) in distant-city wars. The Midianite war applies ḥērem to those who specifically acted against Israel at Peor.
31.19: Purification law for the returning soldiers: anyone who killed (kol hōrēg nep̄eš) or touched a corpse (nōgēaʿ beḥālāl) must remain outside the camp for seven days and purify themselves on the third and seventh days (the standard corpse-impurity purification from Num 19:11-12). Warfare, even divinely commanded warfare, generates corpse-impurity requiring ritual cleansing before re-entry to the holy camp.
31.20: Garments, leather, goat-hair, and wooden articles must also be purified. The comprehensive list covers all materials that could absorb corpse-impurity through contact. The purification principle extends beyond human persons to inanimate objects, reflecting the understanding that impurity is transmissible through physical contact with any material that has been in the presence of the dead.
31.21: Eleazar pronounces the ordinance for the captured metal: gold, silver, bronze, iron, tin, and lead. The priest rather than Moses delivers this ruling — perhaps because metal purification involves fire, which relates to priestly jurisdiction over the altar. The ruling is explicitly grounded in Mosaic authority: zōʾt ḥuqqat hattôrâ ʾăšer ṣiwwâ YHWH ʾet-Moshe.
31.22: The six metals listed — gold, silver, bronze, iron, tin, lead — represent the full spectrum of metals used in the ancient Near East. Their order (from most precious to least) follows standard ancient metallurgical hierarchy. The list is exhaustive by design: every possible metal article is covered by the purification ruling.
31.23: Items that can withstand fire must be passed through fire and purified with the mê niddâ — the water of impurity (the red-heifer purification water from Num 19). Items that cannot withstand fire must be passed through water only. The dual requirement — fire AND water — for metal items is more stringent than the water-only standard for organic materials, reflecting both the practical durability of metal and its heightened purification requirement as articles from a dedicated people.
31.24: Clothes are washed on the seventh day. After washing, the soldiers are clean and may enter the camp. The seven-day structure aligns with the standard corpse-impurity timeline (Num 19:11-19), and the entry into the camp on the eighth day (implicitly the day after the seventh) parallels the new-beginnings significance of the eighth day throughout the Torah.
31.25: A new divine speech unit (vv25-47) legislating the division of the plunder. The legal structure here — with precise fractional levies — exemplifies the P tradition's careful accounting of holy-war spoil as a matter of covenant principle, not merely military practice.
31.26: Moses, Eleazar, and the tribal leaders are to take a full count (śəʾû ʾet-rōʾš hammalqôaḥ) of all the captured goods — persons and animals. מַלְקוֹחַ (malqôaḥ) — capture/spoil taken by tongs/grasp — is the technical term for items taken in combat as opposed to items purchased or inherited. The count is to be precise and documented before distribution.
31.27: wəḥāṣîtā ʾet-hammalqôaḥ bên tōp̄əśê hammilyāmâ... ûḇên kol-hāʿēḏâ — divide the plunder equally between the fighters and the whole congregation. The 50-50 split follows the principle that the community that cannot fight equally shares in the victory — the same principle behind Joshua's distribution of Transjordanian plunder to the tribes that stayed behind (Josh 22:8) and David's ordinance after the Ziklag raid (1 Sam 30:24-25).
31.28: From the warriors' 50%, a tribute to YHWH: one out of every 500 persons, cattle, donkeys, and sheep. מֶכֶס (mekes) — levy, tribute — appears only in this chapter in the Torah, cognate to Akkadian miksu (toll/customs duty). The 1/500 rate (0.2%) is the warriors' tribute because they were the proximate agents of the victory; their tithe is proportionally smaller than the community's.
31.29: The warriors' tribute is given to Eleazar the priest as a tərûmah laYHWH — a contribution lifted up to YHWH. The category tərûmâ — lifted contribution — connects spoil-distribution to the regular offering system (Lev 7:14; Num 15:19-21), integrating military victory into the same framework of returning the firstfruits to YHWH.
31.30: From the congregation's 50%, a 1/50 levy (2%) — five times the warriors' rate. This higher proportion reflects that the community received their share without personal risk; they owe more precisely because they gave less. The congregation's levy goes to the Levites (lal·wiyyim šōmərê mišmeret miškan YHWH — the Levites who keep the charge of YHWH's dwelling).
31.31: wayyaʿaś Moshe wəʾelʿāzār hakkōhēn kaʾăšer ṣiwwâ YHWH ʾet-Moshe — Moses and Eleazar did exactly as YHWH commanded Moses. The compliance formula closes the command-unit; the distribution proceeds by exact divine instruction. The passive voice of the formula — they did as commanded — emphasizes obedience rather than initiative.
31.32: The spoil count begins. The precision of the figures (six hundred seventy-five thousand sheep/goats) reflects either careful military accounting or the stylized large-number conventions of ancient Near Eastern military records. Scholars debate whether these numbers are literal or symbolic/representative; the text presents them as the actual enumerated result of the divinely commanded levy.
31.33: Seventy-two thousand cattle. The four categories of plunder — sheep, cattle, donkeys, and persons — follow the standard ancient inventory sequence, moving from the most common livestock to draft animals to human captives.
31.34: Sixty-one thousand donkeys. חֲמוֹר (ḥamôr) — donkey — was a primary draft and transport animal in the ancient Near East; the number represents significant transportation and agricultural capacity captured from Midian.
31.35: Thirty-two thousand young women (nep̄eš ʾāḏām minhannāšîm ʾăšer lōʾ yāḏəʿû miškāḇ zākār — persons from among the women who had not known lying with a male). The legal language of v18 is here applied as the criterion for the enumeration: only those not involved in the Peor seduction were preserved. The number is recorded alongside the livestock — not because persons are equated with animals but because the same distribution law applies to all categories of captured property.
31.36: The warriors' half of the sheep: 337,500. The precise halving of the total confirms the 50-50 distribution principle stated in v27 — the narrator demonstrates the principle was actually applied, not merely proclaimed.
31.37: The YHWH tribute from the warriors' sheep: 675 (1/500 of 337,500 = 675). The exactness of the calculation demonstrates the precision of the Mosaic law's application; the 1/500 ratio yields a whole number confirming the mathematical coherence of the levy system.
31.38: From 36,000 cattle in the warriors' half, the YHWH tribute is 72 (exactly 1/500). The consistent precision across all four categories — sheep, cattle, donkeys, persons — establishes the levy as a systematic proportional tax, not an approximation.
31.39: From 30,500 donkeys in the warriors' half, the YHWH tribute is 61 (exactly 1/500). The fractional precision of these calculations across large numbers suggests either exact accounting or that the figures were set to yield whole-number tithes — a possibility consistent with round-number conventions in ancient military records.
31.40: From 16,000 persons in the warriors' half, the YHWH tribute is 32 (exactly 1/500). The persons given as tribute to the priest enter the sanctuary system, likely as temple servants (nətînîm), the class of dedicated servants mentioned in Ezra-Nehemiah and attributed to Mosaic origin (Ezra 8:20).
31.41: Moses transfers the priestly tribute (mekes tərûmat YHWH — the levy as YHWH's contribution) to Eleazar. The compliance formula mirrors v31, closing the warriors' distribution unit. Each stage of the distribution is documented and attributed to explicit divine command.
31.42: The congregation's half is separated by Moses — hēḇdîl Moshe — from the warriors' share. The formal separation act mirrors the havdalah principle (separation of sacred categories) throughout the Torah: the two halves must not be confused or merged; their different levy rates require distinct accounting.
31.43: The congregation's half of sheep: 337,500. Identical to the warriors' half, confirming the exact 50-50 split. The repetition of the numbers for each category serves the legal record: the document establishes what each party received, not merely what the total was.
31.44: The congregation's half of cattle: 36,000. The ledger continues with the same precision as the warriors' half, building a complete written accounting of the distribution — a formal administrative record of covenant compliance.
31.45: The congregation's half of donkeys: 30,500. As with the warriors' half (v39), the congregation receives exactly the same number of each category.
31.46: The congregation's half of persons: 16,000. The persons are divided with the same precision as the animals; the distribution follows the same legal category. The Levites receive 320 of these (1/50 of 16,000), matching v30's prescribed rate exactly.
31.47: wayyiqaḥ Moshe miḥăṣî bənê yiśrāʾēl ʾet-hāʾeḥāḏ ʾāḥuz min-haḥamiššîm — Moses took from the Israelites' half one out of every fifty. The 1/50 from the congregation's portion goes to the Levites, completing the levy structure. The Levites receive more proportionally (2% vs. 0.2%) but from the community's larger base, yielding a larger absolute number — consistent with the Levites' role as the permanent servants of the sanctuary.
31.48: The military officers — commanders of thousands and hundreds — approach Moses with an unexpected report. Their voluntary approach after the distribution is complete signals that what follows is not a commanded action but an initiative of the officers themselves.
31.49: The report: every man under their command has been counted (p̄āqaḏnû ʾet-ʿăḇāḏê hammilḥāmâ), and not one man is missing (wəlōʾ-nip̄qaḏ mimmennû ʾîš). Zero battlefield casualties among twelve thousand soldiers in a campaign against five Midianite kings and their forces. The officers recognize this as an extraordinary divine protection.
31.50: In response to the miraculous preservation, the officers bring a voluntary offering: gold articles — armlets (ʾeṣʿāḏâ), bracelets (ṣāmiḏ), signet rings (ṭabbāʿat), earrings (ʿāgîl), and pendants (kûmāz). The stated purpose: lekappēr ʿal-nap̄šōtênû — to make atonement for our lives/souls. Even without personal combat death, the officers recognize that warfare itself, and perhaps their involvement in the expanded killing of vv17-18, requires atonement.
31.51: Moses and Eleazar receive the gold — all worked articles (kol-kelî maʿăśeh). The detailed specification of worked gold distinguishes it from raw gold; the articles are identifiable objects carrying cultural and personal histories from the Midianites who wore them, now consecrated to YHWH's purposes.
31.52: The total: sixteen thousand seven hundred fifty shekels of gold from the officers' voluntary contribution. The precision of the weight — not an approximate round number — reinforces the administrative thoroughness of the record. The large quantity reflects the scale of the Midianite wealth that was captured.
31.53: ʾanšê hazzāḇāʾ bāzəzû ʾîš lô — the rank-and-file soldiers had each taken spoil for themselves. The officers' voluntary offering is distinguished from personal spoil retained by individual soldiers under the provisions of vv27-47. The distinction between the communal distribution (required) and the officers' voluntary offering (spontaneous) frames the latter as above-and-beyond covenant devotion.
31.54: Moses and Eleazar bring the gold into the tent of meeting as a zikkārôn liḇnê yiśrāʾēl lip̄nê YHWH — a memorial for the Israelites before YHWH. זִכָּרוֹן (zikkārôn) — memorial/reminder — frames the gold offering not as payment for past service but as a permanent covenant-reminder object. The tent of meeting functions as the repository of covenant memory (cf. the jar of manna, Aaron's rod, the tablets) — objects that embody the history of YHWH's acts among his people.

34.1: YHWH commands Moses to give Israel the precise borders of the land before entry. The land is not undefined space — it is a named, bounded inheritance (nachalah) with specific geography. God had promised Abraham 'this land' (Gen 15:18); now he delivers coordinates.
34.2: 'Command the Israelites and say: when you enter the land of Canaan, this is the land that shall fall to you as inheritance (nachalah), the land of Canaan according to its borders (gevuloteha).' Gevul (border, boundary) frames all that follows — the gift has edges, which means the gift is real and specific, not merely symbolic.
34.3: The southern boundary begins at the wilderness of Zin along the border of Edom. The south is defined by known terrain — Zin (the desert where Miriam died, ch20) and Edom (the nation that refused passage, ch20:14-21). Even hostile terrain becomes a boundary marker for God's gift.
34.4: The border turns from the south of the Ascent of Akrabbim, passes through Zin, and its limit shall be south of Kadesh-barnea. Kadesh — where the spies reported and the people rebelled (ch13-14) — now marks a corner of the inheritance. The place of failure becomes a border post of the promised land.
34.5: From Kadesh-barnea it goes to Hazar-addar and continues to Azmon. The specificity is remarkable — these are not approximations but demarcated points, suggesting a real administrative boundary Israel was expected to recognize and maintain.
34.6: The western boundary (yam, sea) is simply 'the Great Sea' — the Mediterranean. The sea itself is the boundary, natural and absolute. Israel is not a seafaring nation in the Mosaic vision; the horizon of the sea marks the western limit of the inheritance.
34.7: The northern boundary: from the Great Sea to Mount Hor. This Hor is different from the Hor in Edom where Aaron died (ch20:22) — geographical terminology reused for a different location, a reminder that names recur in Scripture without always denoting the same place.
34.8: From Mount Hor to Lebo-hamath. Lebo-hamath ('entrance of Hamath') marks the northern gateway into Aramean territory. This boundary anticipates the later Davidic empire's extent; it is the promise in geographic form.
34.9: The boundary ends at Zedad, then continues to Ziphron, and its limit shall be Hazar-enan. These northern points anchor the promise in real geography, known to ancient Near Eastern cartographers though obscure to modern readers.
34.10: The eastern boundary from Hazar-enan to Shepham. The east side of the promised land runs through territory contested between Aram and Israel in later centuries. The boundary is not merely legal — it is eschatological, projecting what Israel's inheritance should ultimately encompass.
34.11: From Shepham the border descends to Riblah on the east side of Ain, then down to the shoulder of the Sea of Chinnereth (the Sea of Galilee). Riblah reappears in Jeremiah as the site of Judah's judgment (Jer 39:5-6) — the same points that mark the promised inheritance later mark exile.
34.12: The border continues down the Jordan and ends at the Salt Sea (Dead Sea). 'This is the land with its borders all around.' The Jordan, from Galilee to the Dead Sea, forms the eastern boundary. The river that Israel crossed miraculously (Josh 3) becomes the permanent eastern marker of the inheritance.
34.13: Moses commands: 'This is the land you shall apportion by lot among yourselves, which YHWH has commanded to give to nine and a half tribes.' Goral (lot) signals divine sovereignty — the size of each tribe's portion is ultimately God's determination, not human negotiation. Lot-casting in the Ancient Near East was understood as divine decision.
34.14: Reuben, Gad, and the half-tribe of Manasseh have received their inheritance east of the Jordan already (ch32). Their decision to settle Transjordan, born of pragmatism (good pastureland), means they receive outside the boundaries just described. The Transjordanian tribes exist in a kind of borderland status throughout Israel's history.
34.15: Two and a half tribes received east of the Jordan at Jericho, toward the sunrise. 'Toward the sunrise' (mizrach hashemesh) — the east, the direction of dawn, frames the Transjordanian settlement not as inferior but as oriented toward light. Yet the full promise of Canaan remained west of the Jordan.
34.16: YHWH speaks: 'These are the names of the men who shall apportion the land among you.' The process of inheritance distribution is not improvised — YHWH designates specific leaders. The land-gift comes with administrative structure, not chaos.
34.17: Eleazar the priest and Joshua son of Nun will lead the apportionment. The pairing is significant: priestly authority (Eleazar) and military-civil authority (Joshua) together manage the inheritance. Neither alone — sacred and civil together serve the distribution of God's gift.
34.18: One leader from each tribe shall be appointed to apportion the land. The process is tribal, representative, and inclusive. Every tribe has a voice in receiving what God promised their ancestor.
34.19: From the tribe of Judah: Caleb son of Jephunneh. Caleb survives from the spy generation (ch13-14) specifically because he 'followed YHWH wholeheartedly' (14:24). His presence here as Judah's representative is a quiet vindication — the faithful outlast the unfaithful.
34.20: From Simeon: Shemuel son of Ammihud. Simeon's tribe would later be absorbed largely into Judah (Josh 19:1), receiving no distinct territory. Yet here they stand equally in the apportionment process — the future diminishment is not yet written.
34.21: From Benjamin: Elidad son of Kislon. Benjamin's territory would later contain Jerusalem (on its border with Judah) and Saul's hometown, Gibeah. The small tribe with large significance.
34.22: From the tribe of Dan: Bukki son of Jogli. Dan's territory would prove difficult to hold — the tribe would eventually migrate north (Judg 18), partly explaining why Dan is absent from the tribal listing in Revelation 7.
34.23: From Manasseh (the half-tribe west of Jordan): Hanniel son of Ephod. The full tribe of Manasseh is split — half east (already settled, v14-15) and half west (still to be apportioned). Hanniel represents the western half.
34.24: From Ephraim: Kemuel son of Shiphtan. Ephraim — the younger son whom Jacob blessed ahead of Manasseh (Gen 48) — receives the larger territory in the central highlands and gives the region its central significance.
34.25: From Zebulun: Elizaphan son of Parnach. Zebulun's territory in the Galilee region would become notable in Isaiah's 'land of Zebulun' prophecy (Isa 9:1) and its fulfillment in Jesus's Galilean ministry (Matt 4:13-16).
34.26: From Issachar: Paltiel son of Azzan. Issachar settles in the Jezreel Valley — fertile agricultural land. The tribe is later characterized as 'understanding the times' (1 Chr 12:32), suggesting wisdom alongside material blessing.
34.27: From Asher: Ahihud son of Shelomi. Asher's coastal territory in the northern Galilee would be associated with maritime trade. The prophetess Anna (Luke 2:36) was from Asher — the tribe persisting to the New Testament era.
